facts.fact: Name: Anna Liebig
Age: 14
Birth location: London
Gender: Woman
Person's name: Anna Liebig
Born in: London
Current age: 14

Learned about: The radiator means something is about to happen — The sitting room radiator knocked like a fist every evening when it kicked on, and the sound came before the warmth, and she flinched before she understood why. Her body learned the pattern before her mind did: the knock, then the heat, then the slow warming of the room. Cause and effect before she had words for either.

Learned about: The blue smell means the arms are leaving — Marcus's Head & Shoulders shampoo, the blue bottle in the bathroom, the sharp chemical scent of it on his hair in the mornings — she didn't know 'shampoo' or 'work' but she knew the smell meant the steady arms were about to disappear for hours, and the anxious arms would take over.

Learned about: Czech sounds are home sounds — Klára and Markéta filled the flat with Czech before Anna ever heard English from the outside world. The high voices, the fast rhythm, the questions going up at the end — she didn't know it was a language. She thought it was just what Mum sounded like, what the flat sounded like, what safety sounded like before she knew what safety was.

Opinion: The radiator is alive and angry (because: It knocks like a fist and it comes when the flat gets cold and it's loud and unpredictable and she can't see what's making the sound, just hears it, just flinches)

Opinion: Mum's face means something is wrong even when nothing is happening (because: The tight mouth, the held breath, the way Klára looks at her like she's waiting for the baby to stop breathing — even a baby can read that expression, can feel the anxiety in the arms that hold her)

Opinion: The chest that rumbles low is safer than the chest that vibrates high (because: Marcus's voice goes low and his body is relaxed and the rumble feels like the opposite of the radiator's knocking. Mum's voice goes high and tight and her body hums with something that isn't calm.)

Language: says 'The rise at the end of Czech questions' from Klára and Markéta talking — questions went up at the end, statements went down, the musicality of Czech entering her ears before she knew it was different from the noise outside

Language: says 'The soft 'šššš' hush' from Klára's anxious hush — the Czech way, softer than English 'shhh', drawn out, used when Anna cried but also when Klára was trying to calm herself, Anna would always associate this sound with being worried-about rather than soothed

Language: says 'The low rumble of English singing' from Marcus in the bathroom — 'Yellow Submarine,' 'Hey Jude,' the Beatles because that's what his dad played, his voice going low and silly and different from the high Czech sounds, she didn't know it was English yet, just that it came from a different place

Learned about: How to make 'more' happen — She said the word and biscuits appeared. She said the word and Dad laughed. She said the word and the swing went higher. The most powerful word in any language was English and it was the only one she knew.

Learned about: The hoover means run — The sound of the plug being pulled from the cupboard was enough. Her body learned before her mind. The Henry hoover with the smiling face was a liar and she would never trust it.

Learned about: Pavement cracks make rivers from up high — Dad's shoulders gave her a new world. Things looked different from up there. The cracks she'd never noticed from below were everywhere, running like veins through the pavement.
Opinion: There is a man inside the radiator (because: It knocks like fists and fists belong to hands and hands belong to people and she can hear him, she can HEAR him, why does nobody else hear him)

Opinion: The hoover is alive and it's lying about its face (because: The smile on Henry's face is fake, you can tell, it doesn't match the sound it makes, nothing that sounds like that would smile like that, it's a trick)

Opinion: English is the good language (because: English made Dad laugh. English made biscuits happen. English made the swing go higher. Czech made Mum's face go tight. Czech made the tap run for too long. She didn't choose English. It chose her.)

Opinion: High up is safer than low down (because: Dad's shoulders. The radiator couldn't reach her there. The hoover was a distant sound. The anxious arms couldn't hold her too tight. High up was where the good things were.)

Language: says ''More' — first word, used for everything, said with reaching hands' from From Dad giving her McVitie's Digestives and her reaching and him saying 'do you want more?' until the word became hers, became the key to everything

Language: says 'Answering Czech with English, always English, the wrong language on purpose or not on purpose' from From Peppa Pig, from Mr Tumble, from the park, from the nursery kids, from Dad's singing, from the whole English world pushing in through every crack in the flat like the cold air that made the radiator knock

Language: says 'Saying 'man inside' about the radiator — her own construction, her own logic' from From the knocking that sounded like fists, from not understanding pipes or boilers or central heating, from the way her brain made sense of sounds that had no visible source — if it knocks like hands then hands must be making it

Learned about: Completion is a drug — the feeling of finishing something (a sticker book, a collection, a episode) is the best feeling, and the emptiness after is unbearable — The WHSmith sticker books. The Peppa Pig episodes watched in loops. The Nike shoebox filling with stones. The pattern started here and she didn't know it was a pattern yet, just that finishing things felt like breathing and being done felt like drowning.

Learned about: Things that are yours can be taken by people who don't understand they matter — The stone. The blue-black stone in the crack in the driveway. Mum threw it away. Said sorry. Sorry didn't bring it back.

Learned about: Repetition is comfort — watching the same thing, eating the same thing, doing the same thing — Peppa Pig on loop. Beans on toast every morning. Nando's every Sunday. The same booth. The same order. The bump on the fridge. The 'proboha.' The predictability was the safety. She didn't know she was building a pattern. She just knew that new things felt dangerous and known things felt like air.

Opinion: Wanting more is good (because Dad said so, because Dad brought the activity books, because 'more' was the first word and it always made good things happen) (because: Marcus never said 'that's enough.' Marcus never said 'you've had too many stickers.' Marcus said 'we'll get another one tomorrow' and the promise of more was the only thing that eased the emptiness of being done.)

Opinion: Mum doesn't understand what matters (because she threw away the stone, because she thought it was rubbish, because she looks at things and doesn't see them the way Anna sees them) (because: The stone was special and Mum couldn't see it. The Peppa DVD broke and Mum's face went tight instead of fixing it. Mum bumped the same fridge every day and never learned where it was. Mum moved through the world without seeing it and Anna couldn't trust her to protect what mattered.)

Opinion: The damp spot is alive and growing (because it got bigger, because the smell got worse, because nobody fixed it, because it was like the radiator — another thing in the flat that knocked and grew and nobody did anything about) (because: The bathroom wall had a face. She didn't tell anyone this. The dark patch near the shower grout spread week by week and the smell was like Babička's basement and she thought if it kept growing it would eat the whole flat. Nobody else seemed worried. She was always worried.)

Language: says ''Proboha' — used it like English, thought it was just what you said when you bumped into things' from Klára said it every single day when she hit the fridge. Every. Single. Day. Anna thought it was the English word for ouch or for the fridge or for being tired. She didn't know it was Czech for 'for God's sake' until Markéta Dvořáková laughed when Anna said it at her flat and explained. She was six. She felt stupid.

Language: says 'Peppa Pig phrases as incantations — 'muddy puddles,' 'dinosaur,' 'I'm Peppa Pig' — used them like spells, like saying the words would make the world behave' from The DVD. The endless DVD. The episodes watched until the disc physically degraded. She would walk around the flat narrating like the show — 'I'm Anna Pig, this is my little brother George' — even though she didn't have a brother, even though her name wasn't Pig, even though none of it matched but the rhythm of it was the rhythm of safety.

Language: says 'Saying 'more' with reaching hands — still doing this at two, almost three, the word becoming a demand not a request' from The first word. The most powerful word. More stickers. More Peppa. More biscuits. More stones. More Dad. The word was a key that opened doors and she used it constantly, shamelessly, with her whole body reaching toward the thing she wanted.

Learned about: Leaving hurts the leaver too — Saw Mum crying in the car through the nursery window. Didn't understand it then. Understood it forever. The person who goes is also the person who's gone from.

Learned about: Fish don't expect anything from you — The fish tank at Bright Horizons. Hours spent watching the fish move. They didn't ask her to talk. They didn't try to hold her. They just existed nearby and let her exist too. The first thing she chose over people.

Learned about: Your body can do things your mind doesn't decide — The bite. She doesn't remember deciding to bite Krish Patel. Her teeth were in his arm before she knew they'd moved. This terrified her in a way she couldn't articulate — that she could hurt someone without choosing to, that her body had its own will, that the line between her and not-her wasn't as clear as she thought.

Opinion: Nursery is a place you survive, not a place you belong (because: Every morning was a battle. Every pick-up was a rescue. The smell of bananas and disinfectant still makes her stomach drop. Nothing good happened there. Nothing bad enough to tell either. Just hours of not-being, of waiting, of watching the fish and the clock and the car park window.)

Opinion: Crying should be loud or it doesn't count (because: Mum's silent crying in the car was worse than any scream Anna ever produced. Loud crying you could hear coming, you could prepare. Silent crying was a trap, a secret, a thing you weren't supposed to see. She decided — without deciding, just knowing — that if she ever cried, it would be loud. It would take up space. Nobody would miss it. Nobody would watch through glass and feel helpless.)

Opinion: There is something wrong with her specifically (because: Other kids played. Other kids talked. Other kids didn't bite people or sit by fish tanks for hours or scream when their mums left. Something in her was built wrong. She didn't know what. She just knew that the way she was didn't fit the way the room expected her to be, and that mismatch was her fault because it was her body, her teeth, her silence, her wrongness.)

Language: says 'Private sounds — not Czech, not English, just mouth-noises that meant something only to her' from The fish tank hours. The not-playing. The space between the Czech that lived in the flat and the English that lived everywhere else. Her brain, left with no input it wanted, started making output that belonged to neither world. She whispered to herself in sounds that had no translation, told herself stories in a language that didn't exist, and it was the first thing that was entirely hers.

Language: says ''Bude to dobré' — understood it as a lie before she understood it as words' from Klára said it every morning at drop-off. 'Bude to dobré, miláčku.' It will be good, darling. But her voice went high when she said it, and her hands were tense, and nothing about it was good. Anna learned the phrase as the sound Mum made when she was lying, and she believed it was a lie for years.

Language: says 'Peppa Pig phrases used as armor — 'I'm Peppa Pig' said to the nursery workers who asked her name' from She wouldn't say her name. She wouldn't say anything real. But she'd say 'I'm Peppa Pig' like a spell, like putting on a costume, like being someone else was the only way to be someone at all. The nursery workers wrote it down in her file. Klára was embarrassed. Anna was safe behind the pig.

Learned about: Czech sounds different when it's angry — the rhythm changes, the questions aren't questions, the 'proboha' means something darker than a bumped fridge — Hearing Mum and Dad through the bedroom wall after she was supposed to be asleep, the voices going sharp and fast, the sounds she knew from happy contexts turned into weapons she didn't understand

Learned about: Routines can shrink without ending — Nando's didn't stop, it just got smaller, less reliable, and a thing that's smaller is harder to hold onto than a thing that's gone — Sunday after Sunday watching the booth at Nando's feel different, watching Dad look at his phone instead of at them, learning that something can be technically present and functionally absent

Learned about: Collections are control — counting the 47 stones every night was the only thing that felt certain in a flat where things were changing shape — The Nike shoebox, the lining up smallest to biggest, the touching each one, the saying goodnight — it was a ritual and she didn't know the word 'ritual' but she knew the feeling of something that stayed the same when other things didn't

Opinion: Saturday is the good day and Sunday is the one that lies (because: Saturday was Sainsbury's and Mum's calm face and the Twinkle magazine and the booth by the window. Sunday was supposed to be Nando's but Nando's became unreliable, Dad became unreliable, and the gap between what Sunday was supposed to be and what it actually was felt like the gap between the hoover's smile and its sound)

Opinion: Arguments happen in the kitchen after bedtime and they sound different in Czech (because: She heard it through the wall and the Czech went up and down like it was scared and the tap ran for ages after and nobody came to check on her, which meant it was bad enough that they forgot, which meant it was worse than the radiator, which she hadn't thought was possible)

Opinion: Having 47 of something means you're safe (because: 47 stones. Counted every night. Each one in its place. Each one touched. The number didn't change unless she found a new one, and finding a new one was good, it meant more, and more was always good. The number staying the same was also good. It meant nothing was taken. Nothing was lost. Nothing was thrown away by someone who didn't understand.)

Language: says 'Saying 'more' with her whole body — the word plus reaching hands plus the lean toward the thing she wanted' from Still her most powerful word. Still worked on the swing. Still worked on biscuits. Still worked on Dad's attention. She hadn't learned yet that 'more' would stop working on some things, that you could say it and reach for it and the thing would still get smaller.

Language: says ''Proboha' — still thought it was English, still used it when she bumped into things, now also used it as a whisper when she heard the kitchen voices go sharp' from Mum's word, the fridge word, the ouch word — now it was also the I-heard-something-wrong word, a tiny spell she said to herself in bed when the tap was running and the voices had stopped and she needed something to make the quiet feel less like holding your breath

Language: says 'Peppa Pig phrases as comfort scripts — 'I'm Peppa Pig, this is my little brother George' even though she didn't have a brother' from The DVD. The endless DVD. The episodes she could recite. When the kitchen voices got loud she'd whisper Peppa lines into her pillow because the rhythm of them was the rhythm of safe, of predictable, of a story that always ended the same way with everyone laughing

Learned about: How to sit still, line up, put your hand up, wait your turn — the rules of school — Year 1 with Miss Okonkwo. The carpet. The line at the door. The hand up, not shouting out. These weren't restrictions — they were architecture. She liked knowing what came next. The predictability was the opposite of the flat, where nothing knocked when you expected it to.

Learned about: Moshi Monsters — Katsuma, Moshlings, Rox, the whole ecosystem — Her 6th birthday, Mum and Dad got her the membership after she'd been begging for weeks because everyone at school was on it. The family Dell in the corner of the sitting room, the dial-up sound, the loading screen, Katsuma's orange face becoming the thing she thought about during maths. The collecting, the completing — it was the same pattern as the sticker books, the stones, the Peppa episodes. More. More. More. Done. Empty. More.

Learned about: Horrible Histories songs — all of them, but especially the Vikings one — CBBC after school, 3:45, every day, ritual. She learned the songs before she understood the history. 'The Vikings came across the sea' played in her head during silent reading, during dinner, during the walk to school. She'd hum them without noticing and Mum would look at her with that confused face and Anna would stop, embarrassed, like she'd been caught speaking English.

Learned about: Reading — real reading, not just phonics — Miss Okonkwo's reading corner, the baskets of books, the Biff and Chip series that everyone read, but also the non-fiction books about animals that Anna gravitated toward. She read above her level and Miss Okonkwo noticed and moved her up and it was the first time being good at something felt like freedom instead of performance.

Opinion: Rules are good because they tell you what's coming (because: School had rules and school made sense. The flat had no rules and the flat made her flinch. Cause and effect. Sit here, then line up, then go to lunch. Nothing knocked without warning.)

Opinion: Miss Okonkwo is the safest adult who isn't Dad (because: She expected things instead of worrying about them. She didn't hover. Her beads clicked when she nodded and that was a good sound, not like the radiator. She made Anna feel capable and capability felt like the opposite of fragile.)

Opinion: English is the school language and Czech is the home language and they shouldn't mix (because: English worked at school. It got you praised. It got you moved up in reading. Czech worked at home but only just — it made Mum's face soften and then tighten, like the language itself was a reminder that they were different. English was the outside world. Czech was the inside world. The walls between them were the only thing keeping either one standing.)

Opinion: Mum's love is heavy and it's getting heavier (because: The grip on the walk to school. The 'šššš' in the bath. The checking, the hovering, the way Mum watched her like she might stop breathing. It used to feel like safety. Now it felt like a weight she was supposed to carry without complaining. She didn't know how to say 'you're holding too tight' when the reason was love.)

Opinion: Dad is still Dad but the signal is weaker (because: He still said 'alright, Munch?' He still came home. He still existed. But the late shifts, the evenings in front of the telly, the Sunday Nando's that stopped happening — something was draining out of him, or out of the space between him and Mum, and she could feel the static even if she couldn't name it.)

Language: says 'Singing 'the Vikings came across the sea' under her breath during quiet times' from Horrible Histories, CBBC, every day at 3:45. The song became a reflex, something her mouth did when her brain was elsewhere, like the 'proboha' that Mum said at the fridge.

Language: says 'Answering Mum in English without thinking, then watching her face tighten' from The school voice got louder than the home voice. English was the language that worked — it got praise, it got results, it got you moved up in reading. Czech was becoming the language that didn't work, that marked you as different, that made Mum's face do the thing. She didn't choose to stop. It just happened. Like breathing.

Language: says 'Moshi Monsters vocabulary — 'Rox,' 'Moshlings,' 'Katsuma' — used like they were real things' from The membership. The hours on the Dell. The obsession that was the same shape as every obsession before it — the sticker books, the stones, the Peppa episodes. Collect, complete, empty, repeat.

Language: says ''Alright, Munch?' — parroting Dad's greeting back at him' from Dad's voice, low, warm, the rumble. She started saying it because he said it. Then she kept saying it because it made him smile. Then she kept saying it after the smiles got smaller.

Learned about: Arguments have a rhythm — escalation, pause, escalation, silence — Listening through the radiator pipe every night for months. The pattern became predictable: Dad's low voice, Mum's high voice, a pause that felt like holding breath, then both voices at once, then a door slam or a long silence. She learned to track the argument's progress like weather, like she could feel the pressure dropping before the storm hit.

Learned about: Grown-ups have secret lives that only exist after children go to bed — The bathroom crying. The balcony smoking. The kitchen arguments. All of it happened in the dark hours when she was supposed to be asleep, and the discovery that night-time had a whole other version of her mother in it — a version that cried and smoked and said Czech words fast and angry — cracked something open. The flat had a day-self and a night-self and the night-self was the real one.

Learned about: Drawing cats badly but obsessively — She couldn't say why cats. They didn't argue. They didn't cry. They didn't knock like fists or glow orange in the dark. They just existed and let you exist near them, like the fish tank at nursery but warmer, furrier, something she could put on paper and control. Her cats were terrible — lopsided, too many whiskers, eyes that didn't match — but she drew them on every scrap of paper she found. The back of receipts. The margins of homework. A whole stack of cat drawings in her desk drawer. Miss Okonkwo said 'that's lovely, Anna' once and Anna knew it wasn't lovely, it was just what her hands did when her brain needed to be somewhere else.

Learned about: Czech words she could count during arguments — a game that wasn't a game — Lying in bed listening to the pipe, she started counting the Czech words she recognised. Proboha. Ne. Prosím. Počkej. She'd tally them on her fingers under the duvet, adding each one like a data point, like if she collected enough of them the sentence would reveal itself. It never did. She didn't have enough Czech. She had the sounds but not the meaning and the gap between them was the gap between her and her mother, between the flat and whatever country Mum came from, between knowing something was wrong and knowing what it was.

Learned about: The damp spot is getting worse and no one is coming to fix it — She brushed her teeth every night and the dark patch on the bathroom wall was bigger than last month and the smell was like Babička's basement and she started holding her breath while she brushed because the air felt wrong, thick, like breathing in the thing that was growing. She told Mum once and Mum said 'I know, I've called the landlord' and the landlord never came and the spot kept growing and the smell kept getting worse and this was how Anna learned that telling someone about a problem didn't mean the problem would get fixed.

Opinion: The radiator pipe is a spy, not just an angry man (because: It carries voices. It brings secrets into her room. The knocking was one thing but the whispers are another — the pipe is how the kitchen gets into her bedroom, how the arguments cross the border of her sleep, how she knows things she's not supposed to know. The man inside isn't angry. The man inside is listening. The man inside is telling.)

Opinion: Grown-ups are lying when they say everything's fine (because: Mum's face in the morning after a night of crying was always 'everything's fine, Anna' and it was never fine. The tight smile. The red eyes she pretended were from tiredness. Anna didn't believe her. She never believed her again after the bathroom door, after the orange glow. 'Fine' became a word that meant the opposite of fine.)

Opinion: Home is a place with rooms inside rooms that you can't see (because: The bathroom with the running tap. The balcony with the orange glow. The kitchen at midnight. All rooms she wasn't allowed in, rooms that only existed when she was supposed to be asleep. The flat she lived in during the day was smaller than the flat that existed at night. The night-flat was bigger and darker and full of things no one would say out loud.)

Opinion: Cats are better than people because they don't have secrets (because: A cat doesn't cry in the bathroom with the tap running. A cat doesn't smoke on the balcony. A cat doesn't argue in Czech through a pipe in the wall. A cat is just a cat and you can draw it badly and it doesn't care and it doesn't hide and it doesn't make you stand outside a door unable to knock.)

Opinion: If she doesn't ask, she doesn't have to hear the lie (because: Asking 'what's wrong' gets you 'nothing, go back to sleep' and that's a lie and she knows it's a lie and the lie is worse than not knowing. If she stays quiet and counts Czech words and picks at the wallpaper and draws cats, she can live in the space between knowing and being told. The space is thin but it's hers.)

Language: says 'Counting Czech words during arguments — 'proboha, ne, prosím, počkej' — tallying them on her fingers like a secret score' from The radiator pipe. The night arguments. The need to understand what was happening through the only method available — collecting the fragments she could name, hoping they'd form a picture. A survival mechanism disguised as a game.

Language: says 'Saying 'fine' with the same tight mouth Mum used — mimicking the lie without knowing she was mimicking it' from Klára's morning voice after a night of crying. The 'everything's fine, Anna' that Anna started using at school when teachers asked how she was. She didn't know she'd learned it from Mum. She thought it was just what you said.

Language: says ''The man inside' — expanded now to mean the thing that carries secrets, not just the thing that knocks' from Her own construction. The radiator pipe had a man inside and the man knocked and the man also whispered and the man knew things. The metaphor grew because she needed it to grow — 'angry man' didn't cover what the pipe was doing now. 'Spy' was closer but she didn't use that word yet. 'Man inside' was enough.

Language: says 'Answering 'I don't know' when she meant 'I don't want to talk about it'' from A defensive structure built from months of hearing things she couldn't process. 'I don't know' was safer than 'I heard you through the pipe' or 'I saw Mum crying' or 'Why does Dad come home late?' — 'I don't know' was the wall she built around the rooms inside rooms.

Learned about: How to hear what people mean instead of what they say — Mum's taken-back comments. 'He doesn't appreciate what we have' followed by 'forget I said that.' The way Mum's mouth went tight when Dad's name came up. The way Dad said 'alright, Munch?' like a greeting instead of a question. The bathroom sounds that meant sad, the tap that meant crying, the silence too long that meant Mum was trying to stop.

Learned about: A secret language that belongs to only two people — Elara Chung said 'gip' instead of 'pig' by accident and they built a whole country from that mistake. 'Nna' for Anna. 'Arale' for Elara. 'Emoh' for home. The language existed nowhere else. Nobody could enter it. Not the radiator man, not the spare room door, not Mum's tight mouth, not the pipe carrying kitchen arguments into her bedroom.

Learned about: Closed doors mean something even when nobody says what — The spare room door. The knocking. The pause before 'come in.' The way Dad's things migrated slowly — the blue Head & Shoulders, the football boots, the slippers — and each relocated object was a small proof that she was right to feel the shift, that the world had moved one inch and she hadn't imagined it.

Learned about: Fleetwood Mac is what peace sounds like — Mr. Barrett's phone on the windowsill. 'Dreams.' Forty-five minutes of art where nothing knocked and nothing cried and she was just a girl drawing a cat on a roof. She went home and found 'Rumours' on the family computer, played it quiet through the bad speakers, and the songs became hers, became the thing she put on when the pipe got loud.

Opinion: The spare room thing is fine, it's just snoring (because: Dad said so. Dad said it and she needs it to be true because the alternative is a crack that might become a break and she can't hold the flat together if she admits it's already splitting)

Opinion: Elara's flat is safer than hers (because: Nobody cries in the bathroom at Elara's. Nobody has a spare room with a closed door. Elara's mum makes noodles and smiles and the flat smells like sesame oil instead of damp. The comparison isn't fair but she makes it anyway, every time she walks through Elara's door.)

Opinion: Grown-ups lie to protect you from truths you already know (because: The snoring excuse. The 'I'm just tired' excuse. The 'forget I said that' taken-back comments. They think she can't tell. She can always tell. The lie is supposed to be a blanket but it's more like a sheet — thin enough to see through, thin enough to know what's underneath.)

Opinion: Music can fill a room the same way a person can (because: Fleetwood Mac in Mr. Barrett's classroom. The way the songs pushed out the worry for forty-five minutes. The family computer playing 'Dreams' while she lay on the sitting room floor. When the flat felt empty she could fill it with sound and the sound was better than silence because silence was where the pipe lived.)

Language: says 'The backwards language — 'nna' for Anna, 'arale' for Elara, 'emoh' for home' from Elara Chung's accidental 'gip' instead of 'pig' in the sitting room of her flat, the game that became a country, the language that kept the flat's secrets out because the secrets couldn't be translated backwards

Language: says 'Saying 'it's fine' about things that are not fine, flat voice, no eye contact' from The spare room. The snoring excuse. The taken-back comments. She learned to close her own doors this way — not physical doors, but the doors to her face, her voice, the place where worry lived. 'It's fine' was the English version of Mum's 'proboha,' something you said to fill the space where honesty should go.

Language: says 'Dropping Czech words into English sentences without noticing — 'prosím' instead of 'please,' 'ano' instead of 'yes'' from The flat was more Czech now that Dad was in the spare room. Mum's relief at not having to perform English in the evening. The Czech returned when Dad left the room and Anna absorbed it like the wallpaper absorbed damp, without choosing to, without knowing it was happening.

Language: says 'Calling Dad's room 'the spare room' instead of 'Dad's room'' from Because it was the spare room first. Because calling it 'Dad's room' would make it permanent and she wasn't ready for permanent. Because 'the spare room' implied he could move back, that the slippers could return to the front door, that the migration might reverse.

Learned about: Leaving is a verb that happens to you, not with you — Dad didn't ask. Didn't sit her down and explain. Just left, with one bag, on a Saturday, while the Stone Age song played. She learned that the people who love you can make the biggest decisions about your life without including you in the conversation. This knowledge went in deep and stayed.

Learned about: Grief has a schedule and she couldn't control it — The crying came at 3am, not when she chose, not when it made sense, but when her body couldn't hold it anymore. She'd decided crying should be loud but the loud only came in the dark, in the pillow, when Mum came in and they were both loud together and it didn't help, it just meant two people were sad instead of one.

Learned about: Your presence can be a burden you didn't ask to be — Mum's words — 'Dad chose his freedom over us' — had a 'us' in them, and the 'us' meant Anna was part of what Dad left, part of the weight, part of the reason. Mum didn't say 'you' but Anna heard it anyway, heard that her existence was part of the equation, and the guilt of that was a new thing that didn't have a name but lived in her chest next to the sadness.

Learned about: The waiting is worse than the thing — Sunday mornings were a slow climb toward the door, every minute a check of the clock, every car sound a possible Dad, and the relief when he arrived was smaller than the fear had been, and the fear started again the moment he left because next Sunday was already coming.

Opinion: She made him leave by wanting too much (because: The 'more' word. The biscuits. The stickers. The Nando's. The wanting that Dad always answered — maybe she asked for too much, maybe the wanting was the weight that pushed him out, she couldn't find the proof but she couldn't stop looking for it either, going through the list every night at 3am)

Opinion: Crying in front of someone is giving them something they don't deserve (because: She didn't cry when Dad left because crying would have been a gift and he didn't deserve a gift, he was the one leaving, and if she'd cried it would have meant he mattered enough to hurt her and she didn't want him to know he mattered that much)

Opinion: Mum can't be trusted with the truth because she uses it as a weapon and then apologises and then uses it again (because: 'Dad chose his freedom over us,' then 'I'm sorry, forget it,' then the same thing a week later. Mum's apologies were not the end of the sentence, they were a breath before the next iteration. Anna learned to hear the apology as a pause, not a stop.)

Opinion: The bracelet is more real than the promise (because: Dad said 'I'll see you soon' and she didn't believe it, not really, not in her body. Elara gave her a bracelet and she believed it, wore it until it rotted, because objects don't leave, objects don't choose freedom, objects stay on your wrist and mean what they mean)

Language: says 'Saying 'fine' with a shrug when adults ask how she's doing' from The first week after Dad left, every teacher, every dinner lady, every parent at the gate asked 'how are you doing, love' with the soft face, and she learned that 'fine' was the word that made the soft face go away, that ended the conversation, that let her stop performing grief for people who wanted to witness it

Language: says ''Proboha' said more often, picked up from Mum's increase in saying it' from Mum said 'proboha' at everything now — burning the pasta, dropping a spoon, the news on TV, Anna's school bag in the wrong place — and Anna said it too, matching her, and she didn't think about whether it was Czech or English, it was just what you said when the world was too much

Language: says 'Answering 'I don't know' to questions she knew the answer to' from Mum asked 'why are you crying' at 3am and the answer was 'because Dad left and because you said he chose freedom and because I think it's my fault and because I wanted too much' but that was too many words and some of them were Czech and some were English and all of them were true so 'I don't know' was easier, 'I don't know' was the lid on the container

Language: says 'Calling it 'Dad's flat' instead of 'Dad's house' — flat, not home, never home' from He told her the address — a flat in Croydon, she didn't know Croydon, she knew Tooting and the flat above Ladbrokes — and she filed it as 'Dad's flat' in her head, a place he existed, not a place she belonged, the word 'home' was already taken and already broken

Learned about: How to be alone in a place that used to have people in it — Mum's shifts at the building supply shop meant Anna came home to an empty flat three days a week. The key on the string. The sound of her own footsteps on the carpet. The radiator knocking at half four with no one to see her flinch. She learned to fill the silence with CBBC and the silence underneath the CBBC was a different silence than the one that used to live in the flat — it was a chosen silence, not a shared one, and chosen silence felt like control but it also felt like being forgotten.

Learned about: How to watch someone perform love — Sunday Nando's. Every week. Marcus trying too hard — the questions, the laughter at nothing, the 'alright, Munch?' that came out automatic. She started recognising the difference between real attention and performed attention. Real attention was when he forgot to check his phone. Performed attention was everything else. She catalogued his performances the way she used to catalogue stickers — noting each instance, rating its authenticity, building a collection of evidence that the dad she remembered was becoming a character he played on Sundays.

Learned about: That grief makes people tell children things children shouldn't hear — Mum's unfiltered mouth. The details about Siân. The details about the marriage. The way Mum spoke like Anna was a friend at a pub instead of a nine-year-old eating Weetabix. She learned that adults in pain don't edit. She learned to edit for them — nodding, not asking, absorbing, containing. She didn't know the word 'parentification' yet but she was living inside it.

Learned about: The Horrible Histories songs by heart — all of them, even the ones from seasons she didn't like — The after-school routine. CBBC iPlayer on loop. The songs were predictable and predictability was the only currency that spent right anymore. She didn't watch the episodes for the history — she watched for the structure. Verse, chorus, verse, chorus, punchline. The reliable shape of it. She mouthed the words and picked her thumbnails and let the songs fill the flat where the quiet used to live.

Opinion: The bigger bedroom is a punishment wearing a treat's clothes (because: Mum said it was a treat but the room smelled like Dad and the wardrobe still had his hangers in it and moving into someone's absence isn't a gift, it's an eviction notice with nicer handwriting)

Opinion: Siân is responsible even though Anna has never met her (because: Mum says the name like a curse and the name appears when the crying starts and the name is the reason the flat got rearranged and the reason Dad checks his phone under the table and the reason Sunday Nando's feels like a meeting instead of a meal. Siân is the cause. Siân is the villain. Anna doesn't need to meet her to know this.)

Opinion: Sunday Nando's is a lie they're both telling (because: He performs being her dad. She performs being okay. The food is the same and the booth is the same but nothing that matters is the same and they both know it and neither of them says it. The lie is the point. Without the lie, there's no reason to meet.)

Opinion: Mum is using her as a bin for feelings that aren't hers (because: The things Mum says about Dad and Siân — those are adult things. Those are things for a therapist or a friend or a journal. Not for a nine-year-old eating toast. Anna didn't agree to carry this. She didn't consent. But there's no form to sign, no box to tick, no way to opt out of being the audience for your mother's grief.)

Opinion: Being alone after school is better than being alone with someone who's crying (because: The empty flat is quiet but it's her quiet. She controls the TV. She controls the cats she draws. She controls whether the radiator knock makes her flinch. When Mum is home and crying, the flat belongs to the crying. When Mum is at work, the flat belongs to Anna. Ownership matters when you're nine and everything else has been taken.)

Language: says 'Saying 'fine' and 'nothing' and 'yeah' as complete sentences' from Sunday Nando's. The questions Dad asked that she stopped really answering because the answers didn't land anywhere, they just fell into the space between them and dissolved. 'Fine' was a wall. 'Nothing' was a door she closed. 'Yeah' was whatever he needed to hear so he'd stop trying.

Language: says 'The name 'Siân' said internally with a flinch, like a word that bites' from Mum's mouth. The way Klára said it — 'Siân' — with the sharpness on the S and the flatness on the â, like the word itself was the problem. Anna absorbed the flinch. Now the name lives in her body as a small electric shock, even when she just thinks it.

Language: says ''Proboha' said with Mum's exact cadence, same situations, same tiredness' from The fridge bump. The dropped keys. The shopping bag splitting. Anna said it at school once — the dinner lady gave her a weird look — and realised she'd been speaking Czech without noticing, been channelling Mum without choosing to, been becoming the thing she watched.

Language: says 'Mouthing Horrible Histories lyrics instead of singing them' from The flat was too quiet for singing. Singing filled the silence and then the silence came back worse. Mouthing was safe. Mouthing was private. Mouthing was having the song without giving it to the room, because the room might take it and make it mean something else.

Learned about: How to hold a grudge like a stone in your pocket — Mum told her they were moving and Anna reached for the rage because the rage had edges and the sadness didn't. The rage was something she could hold. The rage had a target — Mum, Dad, the flat, the boxes, the whole stupid situation. She learned that anger is easier than grief, cleaner, more directional. You can point anger at someone. Grief just sits on you like weather. She didn't know this was a lesson she was learning. She thought she was just furious. But the pattern was starting — the choosing of rage over sadness, the wearing of fury like armor, and the armor would serve her for years and also trap her for years, but that was a problem for later, for much later, not now, now there was only the stone in the pocket and the hand that wouldn't let go.

Learned about: How to crack a screen and wear it like evidence — The Samsung Galaxy hit the kitchen wall and the web-pattern spread across the glass and she didn't care. She used the cracked phone for months after, reading through the spiderweb, and the cracked-ness became the point — look what I did, look what this made me do, look at the evidence of how much this hurt. She learned that sometimes breaking something is the only way to show what's happening inside you, and sometimes the broken thing is more honest than the whole thing ever was. The crack was a story she could tell without having to explain. 'What happened to your phone?' 'I threw it.' The end. No follow-up questions. No feelings required.

Learned about: Goodbyes are rehearsals for something you can't rehearse — The last Nando's, the last Saturday café, the last day at Elara's — she did all of them correctly, said the right things, hugged the right people, ate the last chips and tied the bracelet and performed the farewell. None of it prepared her for the car, for the motorway, for the way England kept being England outside the window as they drove toward Dover, like nothing had changed, like the world didn't know she was leaving, like the world didn't care. She learned that goodbyes don't work. They're a ritual you complete and then the real thing happens later, alone, in a room that doesn't smell right, and no one is watching, and you finally let yourself feel it, and it's too late to say any of the things you rehearsed.

Opinion: Mum took England away (because: The kitchen table, the cold tea, the sorry face that wasn't sorry enough to change the plan. Mum decided. Mum packed. Mum drove. Anna didn't get a vote. She didn't get a say. She got boxes and a flat in Benešov and 'it'll be good for your Czech' and the Czech was never the point, the point was being asked, the point was being considered, the point was someone giving a single shit about what she wanted.)

Opinion: Dad didn't fight for her (because: He said 'it might be good for you.' He cleared his throat. He ordered garlic bread. He didn't say 'she's not going.' He didn't say 'over my dead body.' He said 'we'll do video calls' and the 'calls' was already a concession, already an acceptance, already the white flag. She would never forgive that sentence. She would replay it for years, looking for the love in it, the fight in it, and finding only the clearing throat and the garlic bread and the bill he paid without arguing.)

Opinion: The cracked phone is a badge, not a wound (because: She threw it. She broke it. She did that. Not the move, not Mum, not Czech — her hand, her arm, her rage made physical. The web-pattern on the screen was proof that she had felt something so big it had to break something, and she wore it cracked for months like evidence. When people asked 'what happened to your phone?' she said 'I threw it' and watched their faces and the watching was the point. The crack was a story. The crack was a way of saying 'I'm not okay' without having to say it.)

Opinion: Goodbyes don't work (because: She said goodbye to Elara and it didn't stick. She said goodbye to the flat and it didn't feel like enough. She said goodbye to Nando's and the chips tasted the same as always and the sameness was an insult. Goodbyes are supposed to make the leaving feel like something but they don't, they just make you perform feelings you're not ready to have, and then the feelings come later, in the wrong place, at the wrong time, when nobody's watching and there's no one to say them to.)

Language: says 'The cracked-screen vocabulary — 'whatever,' 'fine,' 'I don't care' — short words that close doors' from The throw. After the phone hit the wall she started using language like she used the crack — as evidence, as armor. Short sentences when she was uncomfortable, which was always. 'Whatever' as a shield. 'Fine' as a lie. 'I don't care' as the biggest lie. She learned that you can say less and feel more, that silence can be a weapon, that English is good for walls because it doesn't invite follow-up questions the way Czech does.

Language: says 'Refusing Czech — not speaking a single Czech word during the drive, during the ferry, during the first days, like English was the last piece of England she could keep' from The move. The kitchen table. The knowledge that Czech was the language of the place she was going and English was the language of the place she was leaving, and every Czech word she said was a step away from the flat above Ladbrokes and toward whatever came next. She held English like a rope. She didn't speak Czech for the entire drive, the entire ferry, the first week in Benešov, and the silence was deliberate, was political, was the only vote she had.

Language: says ''Proboha' — but weaponised now, said at Mum, not with Mum' from The fridge bump was accidental. The new 'proboha' was aimed. When Mum said 'it'll be okay, Anna,' Anna said 'proboha' like Mum said it — sharp, tired, for God's sake — and she watched Klára flinch and the flinching was the point. She took Mum's word and used it against her. The word had always been about the fridge and the bumping and the not-learning, but now it was about the move, the boxes, the cold tea, and every 'proboha' was a small revenge that didn't make her feel better but she did it anyway.

Learned about: Silence is a survival strategy — The toilet incident taught her that speaking was dangerous, that her mouth could betray her, that the wrong word could turn an entire room against her. The three days of not speaking weren't a choice exactly — more like a reflex, like pulling your hand away from a hot stove. She learned that if she didn't speak, she couldn't be wrong. If she couldn't be wrong, she couldn't be laughed at. The logic was airtight and the cost was invisibility, but invisibility felt safer than exposure, so she chose it, or it chose her, and by December she was so good at being quiet that it had become a second skin.

Learned about: How to read a room without understanding the words — Three months of sitting in classrooms understanding nothing taught her to read bodies instead of language. The way Mrs. Navrátilová's shoulders relaxed when the class was calm. The way the kids in the front row leaned forward when they were interested. The way tension lived in the air before someone got told off. She couldn't follow the Czech but she could follow the energy, and this was a skill she didn't know she was learning, a skill that would make her sharp later, perceptive, the kind of person who knew something was wrong before anyone said it. It started here, in the not-understanding, in the paying-attention-to-everything-else.

Learned about: Czech folk songs she can hum but not name — Babička's kitchen. The humming. Kde domov můj, the national anthem, which Babička sang under her breath on national holidays. Andělíček můj strážníček, the lullaby. Other tunes without names that lived in Babička's throat and moved into Anna's by proximity, by repetition, by the same way she'd learned the radiator's knocking — not consciously, but bodily, so that years later she would hear these songs and her chest would tighten and she wouldn't know why.

Opinion: Speaking is dangerous (because: The toilet incident. The laugh. The sound her mouth made when it produced the wrong word. Every time she opened her mouth something terrible came out, so it was better to keep it closed. The logic of a drowning person — don't breathe in, don't breathe at all, the water will get in.)

Opinion: Czech is the language that hurts (because: English made Dad laugh. English made biscuits happen. Czech made Mum's face go tight. Czech made the kids in class laugh at her. Czech was the word that meant 'wrong' and 'different' and 'angličanka.' She didn't choose to hate Czech. It happened to her the way the cold happened to her — you don't choose to hate the thing that's freezing you.)

Opinion: Babička's kitchen is the only safe place in this country (because: No one laughed at her there. No one expected her to speak. The humming was the opposite of the radiator's knocking — warm sound instead of angry sound, gentle instead of violent. The koláče appeared without asking. The wood smelled like old and safe and the same. The whole country was a war zone and the kitchen was the bunker.)

Opinion: Dad is becoming a person on a screen, not a person in the world (because: The calls got shorter. The background got noisier. His eyes looked away more. The 'alright, Munch?' became a script, not a question. She could feel him becoming smaller, like a photo left in the sun, fading at the edges, and the fear was that one day she'd look at the screen and there'd be nothing there at all, just the crack running through where his left eye used to be.)

Language: says ''Potřebuju na záchod' — said carefully, practiced in her head for hours before saying, and still got it wrong' from Klára drilled this phrase before school started, sitting at the kitchen table in London, making Anna repeat it, 'potřebuju na záchod, potřebuju na záchod,' and Anna said it right thirty times at home and wrong once at school and the once was the one that lived forever.

Language: says 'Pointing instead of speaking — at the toilet, at food in the canteen, at the page when called on in class' from Self-invented survival strategy. The toilet incident proved that Czech was a minefield and her mouth was a liability. Pointing was universal. Pointing couldn't be wrong. Pointing couldn't be laughed at. She became fluent in gesture.

Language: says ''Fine' — the only word she said on FaceTime with Dad, said flat, said small, said like a door closing' from Not from anywhere. Just the only safe answer. Fine wasn't good, which was a lie, and fine wasn't bad, which would make him worry, and fine wasn't true, which was the point — the truth was too big for a cracked screen, too complicated for eleven minutes, too heavy for a Sunday evening.

Language: says 'The word 'angličanka' — said by Tereza, repeated by others, absorbed like a bruise' from Tereza Bártová, second day of school, whispered but not really, the way kids whisper when they want to be heard. The English girl. The foreign girl. The wrong girl. Anna didn't know the word was coming but she felt it arrive, the way you feel a stone thrown at your chest — the impact before the understanding.

Learned about: Casual spoken Czech — the kind textbooks don't teach, the way real people actually talk — Kovy's YouTube videos first — his channel about history and politics, things she didn't fully understand but the rhythm of his voice was teaching her something the classroom couldn't. Then Matěj on Discord — the slang, the shortcuts. She learned 'fakt' before 'opravdu.' She learned 'seš' before 'jsi.' The living language, not the dead one on worksheets.

Learned about: Minecraft as social architecture — building together as conversation — Matěj invited her to his server. She'd played before — the flat above Ladbrokes had a computer, she'd built a dirt house she was secretly proud of — but this was a city. This was someone saying 'what should we build next?' and waiting for her answer. The building didn't require Czech or English, just blocks and clicks and 'sem' and 'tady' and pointing.

Learned about: Running — the discovery that her body had stamina, not speed, and stamina was its own talent — Joined the school athletic club because it didn't require talking. She wasn't fast but she didn't stop. Coach Novotný said 'máš vytrvalost' — you have stamina — and she didn't know if it was a compliment or an observation but she held onto it like a stone in her pocket.

Opinion: Czech is becoming hers, not just Mum's language, not just the anxious language (because: The word 'jezeře' came out right. The laugh came out Czech. The language is moving from outside to inside and she didn't give permission but she's not stopping it. Czech used to mean Mum's tight face and 'proboha.' Now it means Matěj's patience and Kovy's jokes and Mrs. Navrátilová's smile. The language is two things at once and she's learning to hold both.)

Opinion: Running is the only thing that doesn't need translation (because: The track doesn't care if you say 'jezeře' wrong. Your legs don't need grammar. Your lungs don't need vocabulary. You just run and the world gets simpler. It's the closest thing to the fish tank at Bright Horizons — you just exist and nobody asks you to explain.)

Opinion: Mrs. Navrátilová's smile was worth more than every pity look combined (because: The pity looks said 'you poor thing, you can't do this.' The smile said 'you did this.' The smile expected her to succeed. She would run ten miles for another one. She would learn a hundred Czech words. She would sit in the front row and wait.)

Opinion: Babička's kitchen is the safest place in the world and she doesn't entirely deserve it (because: The food appears without asking. The silence is comfortable. Babička doesn't check her face for sadness or hold her too tight. But the safety comes from a move she didn't want, from a country that isn't hers. She can't separate the comfort from the loss. Every koláč tastes a little like England getting further away.)

Language: says ''Fakt' instead of 'opravdu' — the casual Czech that marks you as someone who actually speaks the language' from Kovy's YouTube videos. He said 'fakt' every third sentence and she started saying it before she knew what it replaced. When she said 'fakt?' to Matěj on Discord and he said 'jo, fakt' back, she felt something click — not just understanding but belonging.

Language: says ''Seš' instead of 'jsi' — the shortened spoken form no textbook teaches' from Matěj on Discord. He said 'seš vtipná' when she made a joke about his tower and she heard it as one word, not two. Had to ask him to repeat it twice before she understood it was 'jsi' in disguise. Now she says it without thinking. That's the point — the Czech that comes without thinking is the Czech that's becoming hers.

Language: says 'The Minecraft-Czech hybrid — 'postav to tam' and 'sem ne' and 'je to hotový?' — stripped-down purpose-driven Czech' from Hours on Discord. The Czech of Minecraft is different from school Czech — shorter, faster, full of 'tady' and 'tam.' The first complete Czech sentence she said without hesitation was 'postavme most' — let's build a bridge — and even at ten she felt the weight of that metaphor.

Language: says 'Dropping Czech words into English without noticing — 'that's fakt stupid' and 'she's so blbá'' from The two languages leaking into each other. She doesn't notice until Matěj points it out, laughing, saying 'you just said half-Czech.' The hybrid is its own language, neither English nor Czech, and it's the one that feels most like her even though it's not supposed to exist.

Learned about: Chess — the rules, the strategy, the patience of thinking multiple moves ahead — Děda František taught her at the kitchen table during lockdown, game after game, his big slow hands moving the pieces, his 'dobře, dobře' when she found a good move, the vinyl tablecloth sticking to her elbows, the mint on his breath, hours of it while Babička made tea and the world outside went quiet

Learned about: Czech — the real Czech, not the survival Czech, the Czech that included slang and jokes and the rhythm of sentences that didn't come from textbooks — Immersion during lockdown — Czech YouTube all day, Babička's soap operas in the evening, Matěj's voice in her ear for six hours at a time, the radio in the kitchen, the news Klára watched, the complete absence of English anywhere except the cracked Samsung and the FaceTime calls she kept avoiding. Three months of nothing-but-Czech did what eight months of school couldn't.

Learned about: How to be alone without being lonely — or at least how to practice — Lockdown forced it. The field behind the house at dusk. The attic room with the door pushed shut. The running that nobody asked her to do. She learned that aloneness could be a choice instead of a punishment, that the quiet could be a blanket instead of a threat, that she could be in a room with nobody else and not feel the walls closing in — sometimes. Not always. But sometimes.

Learned about: Czech YouTube — the creators, the memes, the references that every Czech kid her age knew — Endless scrolling on the cracked Samsung. Jirka Král's Among Us videos. Minecraft series she can't name but can quote. The algorithm learning her language before she fully did, feeding her content in Czech until the Czech became content she understood without trying.

Learned about: That some people show love through presence, not words — Děda never said 'I love you.' Děda never asked about her feelings. Děda just sat across from her for hours, moving chess pieces, saying 'dobře, dobře,' being there without demanding anything back. She didn't recognize this as love until much later, until he was gone and she was trying to remember the last thing he'd taught her, and she realized it wasn't chess at all.

Opinion: Quiet can be safe, not just scary (because: Lockdown forced the world to go quiet and the quiet became the only option and she had to survive it, and somewhere between the running and the chess and the Minecraft, the quiet stopped being the thing before the knock and started being the thing that let her hear herself think. The church bells at night still sounded wrong. But the silence between them was becoming hers.)

Opinion: Děda's silence is different from Mum's silence (because: Mum's silence means something is wrong — the held breath, the tight face, the bathroom door closing, the tap running. Děda's silence means he's thinking, or resting, or just existing near you without needing anything. One silence takes up space and the other makes space, and she didn't know silence could be different until she sat across from a man who was quiet without being afraid.)

Opinion: Running is necessary, not optional (because: The field behind the house at 7pm, the rhythm of her feet, the only part of the day that belonged completely to her, the way her lungs burned and her legs ached and the Czech fell away and the English fell away and there was just the body moving through space, no translation required, no anxiety hovering, no questions in any language. Running was the exit and the entrance both. She needed it like air. She would always need it like air.)

Opinion: The house is too small for all of them (because: Babička's house on Na Purkrabství wasn't built for a quarantine, wasn't built for a mother who couldn't stop checking, wasn't built for a girl who needed to not-be-held sometimes. The walls were closer than the flat above Ladbrokes. The stairs creaked and announced everyone's location. There was nowhere to go that someone couldn't find you. She started memorizing which floorboards stayed silent, which routes let her move without being heard, which doors could be closed without attracting attention.)

Opinion: Czech is becoming hers, not just something being done to her (because: The YouTube. The Minecraft. The chess terms. The slang Matěj taught her that her teachers didn't know. The way she dreamed in Czech sometimes now, the way English started feeling clumsy in her mouth when she talked to Dad, the way she understood Babička's soap operas without subtitles. The language was moving from outside her to inside her and she didn't know when it happened, just that one day the Czech felt like breathing instead of like holding her breath.)

Opinion: Missing English is a betrayal she can't help committing (because: Czech was supposed to be the prize, the thing she gained from the move, and she was gaining it, finally, but gaining it meant losing something too, and the something was the sound of Dad's voice in her ear without the delay, the sound of English in her mouth without the accent, the sound of home before home became two different places. She told Matěj she missed it and he said 'já vím' and that helped but it didn't fix it. Nothing fixes it. You just carry both languages and one of them always feels like leaving and the other always feels like arriving and you're never just somewhere, you're always going.)

Language: says ''Dobře, dobře' — said it constantly, to herself, about herself, like a tic or a reassurance' from From Děda František at the chess table. His slow nod when she made a good move, the two words pressed together like they were one word, dobře-dobře, an approval that didn't make a big deal of itself. She started saying it in her head when she finished a run, when she understood a sentence in Czech, when she did anything right. It replaced 'more' as the word that meant good, that meant enough, that meant you did it.

Language: says 'Chess vocabulary in Czech — jezdec, střelec, věž, dáma, šach, mat' from From Děda, from the games, from the way he'd tap each piece and say its name when they first started playing, like he was introducing her to a room full of people. Jezdec was the knight because it rode, střelec was the bishop because it shot, and the names made more sense than the English ones, the pieces became characters with jobs instead of abstract shapes, and she never confused them after that.

Language: says 'Czech internet slang — 'konec', 'gg', 'trapas', words that lived on Discord and in YouTube comments' from From Matěj, from the Discord calls, from the Minecraft server where language was faster and looser than in classrooms. 'Trapas' became her favorite because it meant embarrassment and it sounded like what it was, like something you wanted to erase, and she used it constantly, describing every mistake she made as 'total trapas' in a way that made Matěj laugh.

Language: says 'Answering Mum's Czech with silence instead of English — the new refusal' from From the lockdown, from the suffocation, from the discovery that leaving the room was a sentence and silence was a language and both of them said 'you're too close' more clearly than words ever could. She stopped fighting in English. She just stopped engaging. The silence was louder than the wrong language had ever been.

Language: says ''Tvoje tah' — your turn — said it to herself when she was stuck, when she needed to move, when the waiting felt endless' from From Děda starting every game, his finger pointing at her side of the board, the phrase meaning it was her move, her choice, her responsibility. It became an internal prompt — tvoje tah, Anna, move, do something, you can't just sit here. She said it to herself before runs, before difficult homework, before FaceTime calls with Dad she didn't want to make.

Learned about: Grief doesn't arrive on schedule — The funeral was performance. The 3am crying was real. She learned that the body holds what the face won't show, that the bill always comes, that not-crying at the service doesn't mean you won't cry, it means you'll cry alone in the dark where no one can see you be human. The delay wasn't coldness. It was overload. She didn't know this. She thought she was broken. She wasn't broken. She was slow.

Learned about: Running turns rage into motion — She didn't decide to run. She was walking home from school in January, maybe February, past the sports complex, and her legs started moving faster and she didn't stop them and by the time she reached Babička's street her lungs were burning and her legs were shaking and the grief was still there but it was smaller, manageable, contained in the physical ache instead of floating in her chest with nowhere to land. She started running every day after that. Not training. Not yet. Just running. The rage in her legs had a purpose now and the purpose was forward.

Learned about: Open caskets are a kindness and a violence — Czech funerals. Nobody warned her. Or they warned her in Czech and she didn't understand the specific word — rakev, coffin, the word she'd never needed before. Seeing him was important and seeing him was the worst thing that ever happened to her. His hands. The clean fingernails. The stillness. She learned that some things cannot be unseen and some things shouldn't be. She doesn't know which category the casket falls into. She still sees his hands when she closes her eyes sometimes.

Learned about: Silence is a language — Tereza Bártová sat across from her and said nothing for twenty minutes and it was the kindest thing anyone had done for her since Děda stopped asking how she was feeling and just set up the chess pieces instead. She learned that 'sorry for your loss' is a wall and silence is a bridge. She learned that the people who can sit with you in grief without trying to fix it are the ones worth keeping. She didn't know this was a lesson. She thought it was just lunch.

Opinion: Not-crying is lying and the body always tells on you eventually (because: She performed strength at the funeral and her body waited three days and then destroyed her at 3am. The delay didn't mean she was strong. It meant she was scared. Strong would have been crying in front of everyone. Strong would have been letting them see. She wasn't strong. She was a stone and stones don't feel and she's not a stone and the pretending almost killed her.)

Opinion: Czech funerals are cruel (because: Open casket. Open. The body right there. His hands right there. She understands the tradition — see the person one last time, say goodbye to the real them, not a box — but the real him wasn't in that casket. The real him was in the kitchen with the chess set. The real him was 'tvoje tah' and 'ne tak napjatě' and the slow patience of his breathing when he fell asleep mid-game. The wax figure in the coffin was someone's idea of closure and it gave her nightmares instead.)

Opinion: Tereza Bártová might not be the enemy (because: The silence at the canteen table. The not-saying-sorry. The 'vidíme se' like it was a normal day. Tereza treated her like a person instead of a wound and that was... new. Different. Anna is suspicious of it. She's been burned before — not by Tereza specifically but by the world, by moves, by fathers who become spare rooms, by stones that get thrown away. But the silence felt real and she trusts real things even when she doesn't trust people.)

Opinion: Running is the only thing that makes the room smaller (because: Grief is a room with no doors. Running doesn't open a door but it shrinks the walls a little, makes the air breathable, makes the body too tired to hold the nothing. When she runs she doesn't think about his hands. When she runs she doesn't think about the clean fingernails. When she runs she just runs and the simplicity of that is the closest thing to peace she's found since the chess set closed.)

Opinion: Babička is alone and Anna can't fix it but she can sit next to it (because: The smaller kitchen. The quieter humming. The way Babička moves through the house like she's looking for someone who isn't there. Anna can't bring him back. She can't make the chair less empty. But she can sit at the table and eat the štrúdl and let the silence exist between them the way Tereza let it exist at lunch. This is what she learned from Děda without knowing she was learning it: you don't have to fix people. You just have to be there.)

Language: says ''Tvoje tah' — said to herself when she needs to make a decision, his voice in her head, the closest thing to guidance she has now' from Děda František's chess voice. He said it every time it was her turn — on the board, in the game, in life, though she didn't know that last part until he was gone and she started hearing it when she needed to hear it most.

Language: says ''Ne tak napjatě' — not so tense — said to her own shoulders when she catches herself clenching, a correction, a kindness from the grave' from Děda's coaching voice. He said it when she gripped the pieces too hard, when she leaned too far forward, when she wanted the game to be over faster than it could be. She didn't know it was a life lesson. She knows now.

Language: says 'Dropping Czech into her internal monologue without noticing — 'dobře' for okay, 'proboha' for everything, 'ani ne' for dismissal' from The immersion finally taking. After a year and a half in Benešov the Czech is becoming the first language instead of the second and she doesn't know when that happened. Mum would be relieved. She doesn't want Mum to be relieved. So she doesn't tell anyone that she dreams in Czech now. That she thinks in Czech. That English is becoming the foreign language and this terrifies her more than the grief does.

Language: says ''Sorry for your loss' — said in English with a flat voice, a mockery, a thing she repeats to herself to make it mean nothing' from The girls at school who learned the English phrase specially, who thought saying it in her other language would be kind, who made it worse by trying. She repeats it now like a spell in reverse, a ward against pity, a reminder that most kindness is just performance.

Learned about: Grief doesn't arrive on schedule — it comes late, in the dark, when no one can see — The funeral taught her nothing. The 3am pillow taught her everything. Her body stored the grief somewhere her mind couldn't access and released it when she was alone, in the dark, with no audience to perform for. She didn't know this was normal. She thought she was broken. She would think she was broken for years.

Learned about: How to be invisible at a new school — The back row. The shortest answers. The observation before participation. The canteen table near the bins where nobody wanted to sit. She built invisibility like a skill, like a fort, like a way of moving through the world without being seen, and it worked — nobody noticed her, nobody asked her questions, nobody made her translate herself. The cost of invisibility was loneliness. She didn't count the cost yet.

Learned about: Czech funeral customs — open caskets, koláče, relatives who speak too fast — She lived it. The open casket with the wrong face. The koláče that strangers brought. The relatives from Prague and Plzeň and near Brno who filled the house with fast Czech she couldn't follow. She learned that Czech grief was loud and communal and physical — people hugging, crying, touching the body — and English grief was quiet and private and she didn't know which one she was supposed to do and she did neither and felt like a monster.

Learned about: Predictability is survival — The canteen table. The same seat. The same order of operations — soup, bread, main pushed around the plate. The routine was the point. The repetition was the point. She didn't know she was deepening the pattern that started with Peppa Pig on loop and beans on toast every morning. She just knew that known things felt like air and unknown things felt like drowning.

Learned about: How to hold grief that isn't hers — Mum's bathroom crying. Mum's 'proboha.' Mum's immigrant guilt about leaving for England. Anna sat outside the bathroom door the way she used to sit outside the bathroom door in the flat above Ladbrokes, and the wallpaper in Babička's house was different but the position was the same — back against the wall, knees up, listening to sounds she wasn't supposed to hear. She became the container again. Eleven years old. Holding what wasn't hers to hold. Not asking how to put it down because she didn't know you could put it down.

Opinion: Her emotions are broken — they don't arrive when they're supposed to and they come too late and too much (because: She didn't cry at the funeral. She cried at 3am into a pillow for two hours. The delay felt like evidence of malfunction. Nobody told her delayed grief was normal. She decided she was broken and the decision calcified.)

Opinion: Being invisible is the safest way to survive a new place (because: The back row worked. The canteen table near the bins worked. Silence worked. Nobody noticed her, nobody asked her questions, nobody made her explain herself. The invisibility felt like control and control felt like safety and she didn't know yet that invisibility also meant loneliness.)

Opinion: Funerals are for the living, not the dead, and they're performed wrong (because: The wrong face in the casket. The lilies. The relatives speaking fast Czech she couldn't follow. The performative crying. She didn't feel what she was supposed to feel when she was supposed to feel it and the whole ceremony felt like a play she'd been cast in without reading the script.)

Opinion: The chess set is a gravestone and moving the pieces would be grave-robbing (because: The set sits on the attic shelf and she looks at it every night. The pieces are where he left them — she can't remember if it was her turn or his, the last game, the one where he fell asleep. Moving them would mean continuing without him and she can't continue without him, she can only exist near the evidence that he existed.)

Opinion: Mrs. Navrátilová is different from the other Czech adults (because: No pity. No anxiety. No 'proboha' face. Just a smile that said 'I see you and you're fine.' The recognition was simple and the simplicity was the point. Every other Czech adult in her life carried something — Mum carried grief, Babička carried absence, the relatives carried noise. Mrs. Navrátilová carried nothing. She just saw.)

Language: says 'Saying 'nevím' instead of longer answers — I don't know, shortened, made into a shield' from The new school. The back row. The survival strategy of answering every question with the shortest possible Czech. 'Nevím' became her default — not because she didn't know but because knowing out loud was a risk she wasn't willing to take.

Language: says 'Using 'tvoje tah' — your turn — in her head when she needs to make a decision' from Děda. The chess games. The patient voice. She hears it when she needs to move, to choose, to act. Not his voice exactly — the memory of his voice, which is different, which is hers now, which is the thing he left behind.

Language: says 'Dropping the formal 'vy' with Mrs. Navrátilová in her head — still saying it out loud, but thinking 'ty' — the intimacy of informal Czech that she hasn't earned yet' from The smile. The recognition. The way Mrs. Navrátilová looked at her and saw a student instead of a problem. Anna started thinking of her in the informal in January, maybe February, but she'd never say it out loud because the formal is respect and the informal is trust and trust takes longer than a smile.

Language: says 'Funeral Czech — 'upřímnou soustrast' and 'kondolence' learned by repetition, parroted without feeling' from The house full of relatives. Everyone said 'upřímnou soustrast' to Babička, to Mum, to her, and she said it back without knowing what the weight of the words was supposed to feel like. The phrase became empty through repetition. Like 'proboha.' Like all the Czech words she said because they were expected, not because she meant them.

Language: says 'Calling the canteen 'jidelna' with a flat voice, no article, the Czech way — not 'the canteen,' just 'jidelna,' a place stripped of comfort' from The table near the bins. The sour smell. The daily routine. 'Jidelna' became a single hard word in her mouth, not a place you go to eat, a place you go to survive.

Learned about: Running as mental silence — the rhythm that drowns out the noise — She ran because the school had a team and Babička said it would be good for her to do something with other kids. She kept running because the first time her legs found the pace — that specific pace where breathing becomes rhythm and thought becomes static — her brain shut up for the first time since she could remember. No radiator. No Mum's face. No angličanka. No ta kráska. Just feet and track and pain she chose. She didn't know other people used exercise this way. She didn't know it was a coping mechanism. She just knew that the half-hour after a hard run was the only time she felt like herself, not the version of herself other people saw.

Learned about: Periods are not the end of the world but they are the end of something — The bathroom stall. The pad under the door. The walk of shame back to class with a wad of toilet paper where dignity used to be. Nobody explained this would happen like THIS — Mum had told her about it, technically, in that halting Czech-English way where she couldn't look Anna in the eye, but the telling was clinical and the experience was visceral and the gap between the two was enormous. She learned that her body would do things without her permission. She learned that she would need to carry supplies like a soldier carries ammo. She learned that Tereza Bártová apparently always had a pad and that this fact said something about Tereza that Anna couldn't articulate yet.

Learned about: Being looked at is a kind of theft — The barbecue. Jana's hand on her shoulder. 'Ta kráska.' The touching. The comments. The sudden visibility. She didn't choose to grow three inches or to have cheekbones or to have whatever it was that made adults touch her hair like she was a showroom car. The looking felt like taking — like people were extracting something from her by looking, by commenting, by touching, and she was supposed to be grateful for the extraction because beauty was supposed to be a gift.
