She learned that the world thought her body was a thing it could discuss. She learned that she hated that. She learned that she also, secretly, in the smallest darkest corner of herself, liked it. She learned to hold both of those truths at the same time and not split open from the contradiction.

Learned about: How to sit with someone in silence and have it mean something — Tereza. The canteen. The table near the bins. Twenty minutes of not-talking that was louder than any conversation. She didn't learn this from anyone — it happened by accident, by not-having-anything-to-say and discovering that the not-saying was okay. That some people don't need you to perform. That silence can be a bridge instead of a wall. She didn't know the word 'companionable' but she understood the feeling, and she understood that the feeling was rare, and she understood that Tereza was the only person who'd ever given it to her outside of Děda's chess games and Babička's kitchen.

Opinion: Being called pretty is not a compliment, it's a warning (because: The barbecue. The hands in her hair. 'Ta kráska' like it was the only thing worth mentioning about her. She had a brain. She had a chess set she couldn't play. She had a running time that was getting faster. But what Jana and Mum's friends wanted to talk about was her face and her body and what she'd do to boys someday, and the conversation wasn't about her, it was about what she looked like, and that was a different thing entirely, and it made her feel like a thing instead of a person, and the warning was: this is what the world sees first, this is what matters to them, everything else is secondary)

Opinion: Tereza Bártová is not the same person who laughed at her in the toilet (because: The pad. The silence in the canteen. The way Tereza never mentioned the bathroom again, never used it against her, never collected the debt. People can be more than one thing. The girl who said angličanka and the girl who slid a pad under the door are the same person, and Anna doesn't have to forgive the first one to appreciate the second one, and the contradiction doesn't have to be resolved, it can just exist, like all the other contradictions she carries)

Opinion: Running is the only time she's really herself (because: The track. The rhythm. The way her brain shuts up when her legs are moving. When she runs she's not the English girl or the pretty one or the container for Mum's grief. She's just a body in motion, earning something, choosing the pain. The 1500m time trial — 5:23 — was the first number that belonged to her alone. Not given. Not inherited. Not commented on. Earned.)

Opinion: Mum's comments about her body are a new version of the anxious arms (because: The posture comments. The clothes comments. The 'boys will look at you' comments. It's the same control dressed up as concern. Mum held her too tight as a baby and now she's trying to control how Anna presents to the world, and both things come from the same place — Klára's fear that something bad will happen, Klára's certainty that the world is dangerous, Klára's love that crushes because it doesn't know how to be light. The anxious arms became the commenting mouth. The grip just moved locations.)

Opinion: Bodies are traitors (because: The period arriving without permission. The growing that happened while she wasn't paying attention. The bite that Krish Patel received before she decided to bite. The way her chest changed and suddenly people thought they could touch her hair. Bodies do things without asking. Bodies get noticed without consenting to be noticed. Bodies bleed and grow and betray you into visibility and you're supposed to be grateful because at least you're healthy, at least you're pretty, at least you're — no. The body is not hers. It's a rental. And the landlord keeps changing the terms.)

Language: says ''Ta kráska' — said with bitter irony to herself, never aloud, a curse disguised as a compliment' from Jana at the barbecue, the hand on the shoulder, the smile that was supposed to be kind but felt like a brand. Anna took the phrase and made it a weapon she points at herself when she catches her reflection and feels the small warm feeling under the revulsion. Ta kráska. The beauty. The cage.

Language: says 'Mixed Czech-English with Tereza — 'that's so trap' next to 'to je hrozný,' switching mid-sentence without noticing' from The café on Náměstí. The back corner. Hours of scrolling and talking and the two languages bleeding into each other because Tereza watched English TikTok and Anna thought in Czech now, mostly, and the intersection was a country only they lived in. 'Trap' from English slang meaning embarrassing — Tereza used it wrong and Anna didn't correct her because the wrongness was theirs.

Language: says 'Calling running 'the off switch' — never to anyone, just in her head' from The first time her brain went quiet. The rhythm drowning the noise. She didn't have words for what running did so she made some up. The off switch. Like she was a machine that could be silenced. Like the radiator could stop knocking if she just ran fast enough.

Language: says ''Dobrý' instead of 'good' or 'dobře' — the casual Czech approval that doesn't require the full word' from Karolína Dvořáková after the time trial. 'Dobrý, Liebigová.' Not 'dobře' — that was what Děda said, that was careful, that was chess. 'Dobrý' was faster, looser, earned on a track instead of given across a board. Anna started using it for things that mattered — a good run, a good time, a good silence. Not for everything. Only for things that were actually good.

Learned about: How to want someone who doesn't want you — the specific anatomy of that, the way it lives in your chest and your stomach and the back of your throat at 3am — Ondřej Svoboda. The talent show. The corridor. The hair-flip. The trip. The 'you alright?' The moment Tereza told her he'd asked her out and Anna's face did something she couldn't control and Tereza saw and said 'I said no, obviously.' Six months of wanting someone who didn't know she was alive except as the girl who tripped over her own bag. She learned that wanting doesn't create being-wanted. That attraction isn't reciprocal by default. That you can stare at someone's hands for weeks and they can look through you like glass. She learned it the hard way, which is the only way she ever learns anything.

Learned about: Running as cause and effect — the clean mathematics of effort and result — Training four times a week. Early mornings. The track becoming predictable, measurable, sane. Coach Novotný's split times written on the clipboard, the way the numbers went down week by week if she did the work. It was the opposite of everything else — the opposite of Ondřej, who she couldn't make look at her; the opposite of Dad, who she couldn't make stay; the opposite of Mum, who she couldn't make happy. Running gave her something none of those things could: the guarantee that if she put in the work, something would come back. The radiator knocked and heat came. She ran and she placed. The logic of it was the first logic she'd ever trusted.

Learned about: How to spot when a boy likes you — and how to pretend you haven't noticed — Matěj Král. The stumbling words. The way he found excuses to walk her to class. The 'hezká' said to his mum. The Discord calls where he went quiet in a way he never used to. She learned the signs not because anyone taught her but because the pattern was unmistakable once she saw it — the same pattern she'd performed with Ondřej, watching from corners, finding reasons to be near. Seeing it from the outside was different. Stranger. Uncomfortable. She didn't want Matěj to like her but she didn't know how to un-want it either, so she did what she always did: nothing, and called it fine.

Learned about: Czech funeral customs — open casket, the specific silence, the flowers that smell too sweet — Děda's funeral was last year but the knowledge persisted, changed shape, became something she carried into this year. The open casket. The way the dead look like themselves but wrong, like a painting where the colours are slightly off. The silence that isn't grief but politeness, the way Czech people stand in rows and don't say the thing they're thinking. She didn't cry at the funeral. She still doesn't know if that makes her strong or broken. She cried at 3am for two hours into her pillow and nobody saw and that felt right, that felt like the correct amount, the correct location, the correct audience of nobody.

Opinion: Wanting is dangerous — it makes you visible and vulnerable and stupid (because: Ondřej. The hair-flip. The trip. The 'you alright?' The six months of thinking about someone who never thought about her. She gave away the power the moment she wanted him and he never even knew she'd handed it over and that was the worst part — not the rejection but the invisibility of it, the fact that she'd been consumed by something he hadn't even noticed.)

Opinion: Running is the only thing that makes sense — the only place where effort produces result (because: She trained four times a week and she placed fourth in the championship. Cause and effect. The radiator knocked and heat came. She ran and she placed. The world was chaotic and people were unreliable and wanting things made you weak but running was clean, running was math, running was the one system that hadn't failed her yet.)

Opinion: Being pretty is a trap — it makes people look at you but not see you (because: The comments started this year — Mum's friends, strangers, boys at school. 'Stand up straight.' 'That top is too tight.' 'You could be a model.' Lukáš Fiala saying she was the prettiest girl in school like that was a compliment and not a cage. Matěj looking at her different since she 'got pretty' like she'd changed when she hadn't, like the outside had shifted and everyone decided the inside must have shifted too. Being pretty meant being looked at and being looked at meant being watched and being watched meant being held — not physically, but socially, trapped in the gaze, expected to perform the thing they saw when they looked at her.)

Opinion: Tereza Bártová is the only person who would say no to a boy for her and that means something she can't articulate (because: Girl code. The stupid phrase that shouldn't mean anything but means everything. Tereza could have said yes. Tereza probably wanted to say yes — Anna saw her face when Ondřej walked away, the brief flicker of temptation before the choice locked in. And Tereza chose Anna. Chose loyalty over opportunity. Anna has never been chosen first before. Not by Dad, who chose Siân and the flat in Croydon. Not by Mum, who chose to move them here. Not by anyone, ever, in any way that didn't come with conditions or guilt or the weight of 'I did this for you.' Tereza's 'no' was clean. No debt. No guilt. Just the choice. Anna doesn't know how to thank someone for that so she doesn't try, she just shows up at the café every Wednesday and hopes Tereza understands.)

Language: says 'Mixed Czech-English with Tereza — 'that's so cringe, ale vážně, like proper cringe, šílený'' from Tereza Bártová watches English TikTok, Anna grew up with English, the combination became their private language, nobody else at school can follow it and that's the point, it's a border they control, a country with two citizens

Language: says ''Good for you' — said flat, said Czech, said wrong' from What she said when Tereza told her Ondřej had asked her out. She meant 'thank you for saying no' and she meant 'I hate this' and she meant 'I'm dying' and she said 'good for you' because the other things were too honest and honesty felt like bleeding in public. The phrase has calcified. She says it now when she means the opposite. Tereza knows. That's why it works.

Language: says 'Running terminology used as emotional shorthand — 'splits,' 'personal best,' 'lactic acid'' from From Coach Novotný, from Karolína Dvořáková, from the language of the track. When she can't describe how she feels about Ondřej she says 'bad split.' When something goes right she says 'personal best.' The metaphor is unconscious. She doesn't know she's doing it.

Language: says ''Proboha' — still using it, still Czech, still the word that comes out when she bumps into things or drops something or sees something that makes her stomach drop' from Klára. The fridge. The word that lives in her body before it lives in her mind. She said it once at school and someone laughed and she felt the old shame from when Markéta Dvořáková explained what it meant, but she can't stop saying it, it's not a choice, it's a reflex, it's the sound her mouth makes when the world goes wrong.

Learned about: How to make a house feel smaller by adding one person — Pavel's coat on the hook. Pavel's mug in the cupboard — the blue one with the chip on the rim, she hates that mug, it's always in the sink. Pavel's car in the driveway taking up the space where her running shoes used to dry. Pavel's smell — deodorant and something woody, not Dad's smell, never Dad's smell — in the hallway when she comes downstairs. The house didn't change size. The house just started pressing in.

Learned about: Politeness is its own kind of violence — She said 'dobrý den' to Pavel every morning because Mum asked her to and the word tasted like giving in. She said 'děkuji' when he bought the trainers because not saying it would have been a scene and she didn't want to give him the scene. Every polite word was a small surrender and she felt each one in her chest, a tiny betrayal of the part of her that wanted to scream.

Learned about: The body keeps running when the mind wants to stop — The tenth 400. The legs that wouldn't quit. The lungs that burned and kept breathing. She learned that she could outrun the anger, at least temporarily, at least for the forty-five minutes of a hard session, and the outrunning was the closest thing to peace she had. She didn't know this was a coping mechanism. She just knew that the track was the only place where the house wasn't.

Learned about: Guilt doesn't cancel anger — they just stack — She felt guilty about the fights with Mum and angry about feeling guilty and guilty about being angry and the whole thing was a spiral she couldn't find the top of. Nobody taught her how to hold two feelings at once. Nobody taught her that the love and the suffocation could both be true. She just held them and they were heavy and she couldn't put either one down.

Opinion: Pavel Souček is not her father and never will be (because: He wears slippers in someone else's house. He buys trainers like affection can be purchased. He says 'dobrý den' every morning like he's asking permission to exist in a space that was never his. He's not Dad. He's not anything. He's just there, taking up room, making the house smaller, making Mum's face go soft in a way it never went for Dad, and the softness is worse than the anger because it means Mum has moved on and Anna hasn't and the gap between them is growing.)

Opinion: The house isn't hers anymore (because: The coat on the hook. The mug in the sink. The slippers by the door. The car in the driveway. Every object is a flag planted in territory she thought was safe. Babička's house was supposed to be neutral — not England, not the flat above Ladbrokes, not anywhere that belonged to anyone — and now it belongs to Pavel too, slowly, one object at a time, and she can feel herself being pushed out of rooms she used to sit in.)

Opinion: Gratitude is a trap (because: Mum says 'I'm doing this for us' and the 'us' is supposed to include Anna but it doesn't feel like inclusion, it feels like conscription. She didn't vote for this. She didn't choose the move or the country or the man. She's supposed to be grateful for a life she didn't pick and the gratitude is a cage with nice bars — you can see through them but you can't get out. Saying thank you for the trainers was saying 'okay, you're allowed to be here' and she doesn't think he's allowed to be here but she said it anyway because the alternative was a fight she was too tired to have.)

Opinion: Running is the only thing that's actually hers (because: The track doesn't have a fourth place setting. Coach Novotný doesn't ask her to be polite. The intervals don't care if she's grateful. Running is the only place where she isn't someone's daughter or someone's problem or someone's obligation. It's just a body moving through space at a measurable speed and the measurability is the only honest thing left.)

Opinion: Mum is happier and that's worse somehow (because: The tight face is looser. The 'proboha' comes less often. Klára hums in the kitchen now, actual humming, not the anxious kind. This should be good. Anna knows it should be good. But it isn't good because the happiness has a source and the source is Pavel and Pavel is the problem and how can the problem and the solution be the same person? She can't be happy for her mother without being angry at the cause of the happiness and the contradiction is exhausting and she doesn't know how to hold it.)

Language: says 'Calling Pavel 'on' — the Czech formal 'he' — instead of by name' from Started in January when he began staying over. She refused to say his name in the house because naming him would make him real, would make him permanent. 'On' was distance. 'On' was a wall built out of a pronoun. Klára noticed. Klára said 'you could call him Pavel.' Anna said nothing and kept saying 'on.'

Language: says 'Mixed Czech-English insults — 'he's such a dick, ale vlastně ne, he's just oblivious'' from With Tereza Bártová only. The mix is theirs, a language that belongs to no country, and it's the only way she can talk about Pavel without the words feeling like they belong to him. In English he's 'a dick.' In Czech he's 'on.' Between the two languages there's a space where she can say what she actually means.

Language: says ''Proboha' said at Pavel instead of about the fridge' from The word used to be Mum's, used to belong to the bumping and the anxiety and the small daily frustrations. Now Anna says it under her breath when Pavel's car pulls into the driveway. She doesn't know she's using her mother's language. She doesn't know the word is migrating, becoming hers, becoming the sound she makes when the world is too close.

Language: says 'Saying 'fine' to Dad on FaceTime — one word, flat, a door closing' from Used to be 'alright, Munch?' 'Yeah, good, Dad, how are you?' Now it's 'alright, Munch?' 'Fine.' The word is a wall. The word is the distance between Croydon and Benešov made audible. She doesn't know she's doing it. She doesn't know he can hear the gap growing.

Learned about: A clean life without her in it — The Croydon flat. No knocking radiator, no damp spot, no wallpaper curl, no anxious arms, no spare room that used to be Dad's. The life was real and it worked without her and the knowledge was like finding out you're optional in a story you thought you were the main character of.

Learned about: Nice is harder than mean — Lukáš Fiala said she was pretty and she said 'okay' and walked away. She knew what to do with cruelty — bite it, run from it, store it for later. Kindness was a door she couldn't find the handle for. She replayed the moment for weeks trying to figure out what the right response was and every option felt like a costume that didn't fit.

Learned about: The body is a message she didn't write — Prettiest. He said prettiest. Not fastest, not smartest, not funniest. The word stuck because it confirmed what Mum's friends had been saying, what the strangers noticed, what Klára's anxious arms were trying to protect her from: the body is the first thing people read and she didn't write the words on it.

Learned about: Villains are easier than people — Siân was supposed to be tall and mean. She was short and nervous. Anna had spent years building a villain and the real woman dismantled it in thirty seconds and left nothing to put in its place. You can hate a villain. You can only feel complicated about a person. Complicated is exhausting.

Opinion: Dad's new life is clean and wrong and she wasn't invited (because: The Croydon flat had no knocking radiator, no damp spot, no evidence that difficulty had ever lived there. The nursery had felt clouds on the mobile. The kitchen had a dishwasher that worked. The whole flat was proof that he could build a functional life and he'd done it without her, and the cleanness was an accusation.)

Opinion: Pretty is a trap (because: Lukáš Fiala saw her face and called it the best face in school. Mum's friends see her body and comment on it. Strangers look at her and see something she didn't make. Pretty means being seen. Being seen means being vulnerable. She wants to be fast, not beautiful, because fast is something she earned and pretty is something that happened to her.)

Opinion: Siân is worse than a villain because you can't hate her properly (because: A villain you can fight. Siân touches her coffee stain and laughs nervously and asks about school and is clearly trying to be kind and kindness from the woman who replaced your mum is the worst kind because you can't fight it without becoming the villain yourself.)

Opinion: Poppy didn't do anything wrong and that's the worst part (because: She's three. She has his eyes. She doesn't know that her existence proves something painful. Anna can't be angry at a toddler but the anger has to go somewhere so it goes inward, becomes the 3am crack, becomes the nothing that isn't actually nothing.)

Opinion: Lying is easier than breaking things (because: She told Dad she was happy in Czech Republic. She told Dad she was busy at Christmas. Both lies, both easier than the truth, because the truth would require explaining that the distance isn't just kilometers, it's the clean flat and the felt clouds and the blue eyes that aren't hers, and the explanation would break something she can't afford to break because she's already broken enough.)

Language: says 'Saying 'okay' to everything emotional — compliments, invitations, apologies' from From Lukáš Fiala. The word became a shield after it failed her in the corridor. 'Okay' closes doors. 'Okay' means the conversation is over. 'Okay' means she doesn't have to find the right words because there are no right words.

Language: says 'Dropping 'literally' into Czech sentences — 'já literally nemůžu'' from From the voice note. From English TikTok. From the part of her that still thinks in English when she's stressed. The word slides in without permission and marks her as what she is: a girl between two languages who can't keep them separate anymore.

Language: says 'Calling things 'cringe' in English instead of 'trapnej' — the Czech word exists but it doesn't hit the same' from From TikTok, from Elara Chung's old texts, from the English voice in her head that narrates her embarrassments. 'Cringe' is self-judgment in a language that doesn't belong to anyone in Benešov. It's private. 'Trapnej' is public.

Language: says 'Saying 'I'm fine' in English to herself at 3am, never in Czech' from From Dad. From the flat above Ladbrokes. From a version of herself that still lives in London and handles things in English because English is the language where she learned to lie.

Learned about: True crime podcasts in English — 'My Favorite Murder,' 'Casefile,' 'Morbid' — Started in January because she needed English in her ears, needed the accents, needed the sound of home that wasn't Dad's voice getting smaller through the phone. Found 'My Favorite Murder' first — the two women laughing about death felt wrong and then felt right because laughing about terrible things is how you survive them. Then 'Casefile' for the nights she wanted to be scared of something that wasn't her own life. The narrators' voices are Australian, American, English — accents from a world she left, sounds that make the attic room dissolve for forty minutes at a time. The fear the podcasts give her is clean. It has a beginning, middle, end. Unlike the fear in her stomach about Dad, about Babička, about who she's becoming.

Learned about: Czech rap — Yzomandias, Viktor Sheen, Démo — Matěj Král played Yzomandias on Discord in October, maybe November, she can't remember exactly, just the sound — fast and angry and Czech in a way school Czech wasn't, in a way Babička's Czech wasn't, in a way that felt like it belonged to her generation instead of borrowed from adults. She went home and found Viktor Sheen's 'Černobílej svět' and the lyrics hit something — the black-and-white world, the nowhere feeling, the anger that doesn't have a name. Now she listens before track meets, before fights with Mum, before bed. The bass in her earbuds. The Czech faster than she can think. The anger matching hers beat for beat.

Learned about: How to push through the wall — the specific pain of lactic acid at 600m — Coach Novotný taught the theory but Karolína Dvořáková taught the practice by just being there, running next to her on the 400s, not letting her slow down without saying a word. The body has a point where it wants to quit and the mind has to decide whether to listen. Anna learned that she can override the quit-signal for about twelve more seconds than she thought she could. The knowledge isn't about running. It's about everything.

Learned about: That she's becoming someone her father won't recognise — The FaceTime calls proved it. His face when she used a Czech idiom he didn't know. His confusion when she mentioned the track in Pod Lázni like it was obvious. The way he said 'you sound different' in March, maybe April, and she didn't ask different how because she already knew — different Czech, different confidence, different anger. The England she came from is fading and the Czech she's becoming is hardening and the girl in the middle is unrecognisable to the man who used to be her whole world.

Opinion: Being pretty is a trap — people see it before they see anything else and they decide who you are based on it (because: Lukáš Fiala said 'nejhezčí holka' and she froze because he didn't say smart or funny or fast, he said pretty, and pretty is the thing that gets you noticed and noticed is the thing that makes Mum's face go tight and Mum's face going tight is the thing that makes the anxious arms come back and the anxious arms are the thing she's spent fourteen years trying to escape. Also: strangers on the street, Mum's friends, boys at school — they all see the body first. The body she didn't ask for. The body that's changing without her permission. Pretty is a cage with nice bars, same as gratitude.)

Opinion: The guilt is worse than the thing you feel guilty about (because: Dodging Dad's calls feels bad for five minutes. The guilt about dodging them feels bad for three days. She's learned that avoidance is a short-term relief with long-term interest, and the interest is destroying her, but she keeps doing it because five minutes of relief is five minutes of not-having-to-perform-connection and the performance is exhausting and the alternative is silence and the silence is worse. She doesn't know how to break the cycle. She doesn't know if she wants to.)

Opinion: Running is the only honest thing in her life (because: The track doesn't have opinions about her body. Coach Novotný doesn't care if she's Czech or English. Karolína Dvořáková doesn't ask how she's feeling. The clock doesn't lie. The distance doesn't care about her family. Every other part of her life is complicated and tangled and full of people who want things from her that she can't give — the track just wants her to run, and she can run, and the simplicity of that exchange is the only thing that makes sense some days.)

Opinion: She's never leaving Benešov (because: The thought arrived one night at 3am and she couldn't make it leave. She'll go to university in Prague maybe, but Prague is still Czech, still this life, still this country that chose her when she didn't choose it. The girls who leave are the girls with money, with fathers who show up, with mothers who don't need saving. She's the girl who stays. She's the girl who runs circles on the same track and sits in the same café and watches Babička get older in the same kitchen. The fear is heavy and shapeless and she can't run it off.)

Opinion: Mum is right about everything and that's the worst part (because: About Pavel — he is trying. About England — it was a mess. About Anna's body — boys are looking. About being careful — the world is dangerous for girls who look like her. Every opinion Klára has is grounded in experience and trauma and love and being-right-about-everything is the most suffocating quality a mother can have because you can't argue with right, you can only suffer under it, and Anna knows this and still can't stop arguing, still can't stop using the blade ('I didn't ask you to leave England') even when she knows it's wrong, even when she knows Mum is right, maybe because she knows Mum is right and being right isn't the same as being helpful.)

Language: says 'Dropping Czech words into English sentences without noticing — 'he was being such a debil about it' and 'that's proper hrozný, like genuinely' and 'she's so namyšlená, you know?'' from Four years in Benešov. The Czech isn't just a second language anymore, it's eating the English from the inside, replacing words she used to know with words that feel faster, sharper, more accurate. 'Debil' hits harder than 'idiot.' 'Hrozný' covers more ground than 'terrible.' She doesn't notice she's doing it until Tereza points it out and then she notices it constantly, the mixed-up language of a mixed-up person.

Language: says 'True crime podcast phrases used as dark jokes — 'stay sexy and don't get murdered' from My Favorite Murder, said to Tereza when they walk home in the dark, both of them laughing at something that isn't actually funny' from The podcasts. The hours in the attic with English voices in her ears telling her about women who died. The phrase is armor — if you joke about murder, you can't be murdered. She knows this is irrational. She says it anyway.

Language: says 'Czech rap lyrics as internal monologue — Yzomandias lines running through her head during track, during fights, during the awful silence after she says something cruel' from The anger. The lyrics give her anger a container, a rhythm, a shape. When she can't articulate why she's furious, Viktor Sheen can, and the words aren't hers but they feel like hers, like borrowed weapons that fit her hands perfectly.

Language: says ''Proboha' — still using it, still feeling the phantom shame of being six and not knowing it was Czech' from Klára. The fridge. The bump. The word that was supposed to be English and wasn't. Every time she says it now she hears Markéta Dvořáková laughing and feels six again and stupid again and the word is hers now, truly hers, Czech and natural and automatic, but the ghost of that first shame still lives in it.

Language: says 'Short declarative sentences when she's uncomfortable — 'Fine.' 'Yeah.' 'Okay.' 'No.' — and longer run-on sentences when she's angry, the words piling up like cars in a crash' from The defensive architecture of a girl who learned early that words are weapons and silence is armor. The short sentences keep people out. The long sentences let the anger out. She doesn't have a middle register for normal conversation, just the two speeds — locked door and house fire.

Core memory: The radiator knocking like a fist every evening and nobody else hearing the man inside it (matters because: Taught her that danger exists inside walls, that things can be alive when they shouldn't be, that she perceives things others don't and nobody believes her. The fear of unseen forces started here.)

Core memory: The blue Head & Shoulders smell meaning Dad was leaving — she learned it before she knew 'leaving' or 'work' or 'why' (matters because: Her first experience of cause and effect was abandonment. Smell = loss. The pattern of bracing for absence started before language did.)

Core memory: Mum throwing away the blue-black stone from the driveway crack because it was 'just rubbish' (matters because: Proof that Mum doesn't understand what matters. That love can destroy things by not seeing them. That the people who hold you too tight can also throw away what you love. Still angry. Still.)

Core memory: Seeing Mum crying in the car through the nursery window — silent, shoulders shaking, alone (matters because: She made Mum cry by existing, by needing to be left somewhere. The guilt entered the structure like damp in a wall. Also: silent crying is worse than loud crying. She decided if she ever cried, it would take up space.)

Core memory: Biting Krish Patel — teeth in his arm before she decided to move them — her body acting without her (matters because: The terror of having a body that does things without permission. That the line between her and not-her wasn't solid. She still doesn't trust her own reactions. Still flinches at her own impulses.)

Core memory: Sitting on Dad's shoulders where the radiator couldn't reach her and the cracks in the pavement looked like rivers (matters because: High up was safe. Dad was safe. The world looked different from his shoulders — manageable, beautiful even. Every time she climbs something now, she's looking for that feeling. She never finds it.)

Core memory: Saying 'more' and biscuits appearing, Dad laughing, the swing going higher — English as the good language, the powerful language (matters because: English = making things happen. Czech = Mum's tight face. She didn't choose English. It chose her. The division between her and Mum started in vocabulary.)

Core memory: The damp spot on the bathroom wall growing, smelling like Babička's basement, having a face if you looked right — nobody fixed it, nobody worried (matters because: Things that are ignored get worse. Things that grow in the dark will eat the whole flat if you let them. She learned to track problems because nobody else was tracking them. She's still tracking.)

Core memory: Finding out 'proboha' was Czech, not English — Markéta laughing, Anna feeling stupid at six years old (matters because: The first time she understood she'd been living in a translation error. That her own language wasn't her own. That she didn't know what she was saying. The embarrassment still burns.)

Core memory: The fish tank at Bright Horizons — hours watching things that didn't ask her to talk or try to hold her (matters because: The first time she chose not-people over people. The beginning of a pattern: watching from outside, preferring silence to demand, finding calm in things that don't need anything from her.)

False certainty: believes 'It was raining when she saw Mum crying in the car at Bright Horizons. She can see the rain on the window, the wet car park, the wipers not moving because the engine was off.' but actually 'It wasn't raining. The memory has fused with other drop-offs, other grey mornings, and the rain is a detail her brain added to make the scene feel as heavy as the emotion. Several drop-offs happened in rain. This specific one didn't.'

False certainty: believes 'The stone was blue-black and smooth and fit perfectly in her hand, like it was made for her. She remembers the exact color, the exact texture, the exact shape.' but actually 'The stone was probably just a regular grey piece of gravel from the driveway crack. Four-year-olds don't have reliable color memory, and the stone has been elevated by grief into something more precious and specific than it was. The 'blue-black' is almost certainly a confabulation — there are no blue-black stones native to that driveway, just standard London gravel.'

False certainty: believes 'The radiator knocked every single evening without fail, at exactly the same time, like clockwork. She's certain of the regularity because the regularity was what made it scary — the reliability of the fear.' but actually 'The radiator knocked when the heating kicked on, which depended on the thermostat, the boiler cycle, and whether Klára had turned it up. It probably knocked most evenings but not every one, and not at the same time. Anna's brain has compressed the pattern into something more absolute than it was because absolute patterns feel more controllable than random ones, even when the pattern is threatening.'

False certainty: believes 'She was definitely five, maybe six, when she found out 'proboha' was Czech. She remembers being small enough that the embarrassment was physical, a full-body flush.' but actually 'She was probably closer to four. The embarrassment has aged the memory — it feels bigger, so she assumes she was bigger. But Markéta Dvořáková stopped coming around regularly when Anna was about four and a half, and the interaction likely happened earlier than Anna places it.'

Marcus Liebig.context: family; He became 'the fun one' not by trying but by default — he wasn't the one who flinched at every sound, who checked her breathing twelve times a night, who held her like she might shatter. His hands were steady because they didn't know how to be anything else. When he picked her up she stopped crying faster, and Klára noticed this and never said anything about it, and the distance between them started in that silence.; He became the hero, the fun one, the one who laughed when she said 'more' instead of crying, the shoulders she sat on, the voice that rumbled low and safe. He didn't flinch when she made noise. He didn't check her breathing. He just held her and walked and pointed at things and gave her biscuits and made her feel like the world was giving her things instead of taking them. Every English word was a victory with Dad. Every English word was a loss with Mum. He didn't create that division. He just lived on one side of it.; He became the bringer. The activity books, the Nando's, the 'more.' When she wanted something, he provided it. Not always perfectly — he didn't look at the stickers, he watched the football — but he showed up with things. He made wanting feel safe. She started waiting for the front door to open because the door meant Dad and Dad meant something new to complete, something new to love too much.; The absences started like weather — you don't notice the first grey cloud, you notice when the whole sky changes. Late shifts became later shifts. Sunday Nando's became sometimes-Sunday Nando's. The front door opening at 6pm became the front door opening at 8pm became the front door not opening until after she was in bed. He was still there. He was just less there. She didn't know to miss him yet because she hadn't learned that someone could be physically present and emotionally absent — that vocabulary would come later. But her body knew. Her body noticed the quiet nights, the fewer rumbles through the wall, the Sundays where the booth at Nando's felt too big for three people.; He was becoming a radio signal losing strength — still broadcasting, still there, but the static was eating the music. The late shifts were the visible thing but the real thing was the way he came home and sat in front of the television instead of sitting on the floor with her. The way 'alright, Munch?' became a greeting instead of a question. The way Sunday Nando's started getting cancelled — not cancelled, rescheduled, and then not rescheduled, just quietly dropped like a stone into water. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She didn't have the word for this yet. She would.; He stopped being the one who was there and became the one who might be there. The front door sound used to mean Dad, meant biscuits, meant the low rumble, meant 'more.' Now the front door sound was a question she asked herself every evening, and the answer was 'no' more often, and the disappointment collected like dust on surfaces she couldn't reach. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She started protecting herself by expecting nothing and then he'd come home early with a magazine and she'd feel it all at once — the relief and the anger and the confusion of loving someone who was becoming unreliable. She didn't have the word 'unreliable.' She had the feeling of waiting.; He became the far one. Not gone — the spare room was still in the flat, still just down the hallway — but separated by a door she now had to knock on, and the knocking changed everything. The pause before he said 'come in.' The way his room smelled only like him now, not like Mum, not like their shared life. The football boots by the wrong door. The slippers. The gradual migration of a person she'd assumed was permanent. Sunday Nando's became unreliable. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She started preparing for the closed door before she reached it, already rehearsing the knock, already expecting the pause, and the preparation was its own kind of grief — the grief of adjusting to less of someone who was still alive, still home, still technically there.; He became someone who visited. The distance changed from down-the-hall to across-London, and the man who arrived for Sunday Nando's was trying so hard to be the fun one that he was performing, and she could feel the performance even if she couldn't name it. He still ordered too much. He still said 'alright, Munch?' But the ease was gone — replaced by effort, by checking his phone, by the way he asked about Mum and then didn't listen to the answer. She started protecting herself by expecting less, by not hoping, by treating Sundays like appointments instead of visits. The fear that he'd forget to come lived in her stomach every Sunday morning and never left even though he never forgot, not once, and she couldn't explain why the fear didn't go away when the evidence said it should.; He became someone she watched instead of someone she was with. The Sunday visits were structured, rehearsed, full of questions he asked because he was supposed to, not because he wanted the answers. She started tracking his attention — when he checked his phone, when his eyes drifted to the TV above the counter, when he said 'that's class' about something she'd said that wasn't class-worthy, it was just a thing, and the emptiness of his praise made her stop sharing. She protected herself by expecting less. By the time summer came, she'd perfected the art of filling silences with chip-eating so he wouldn't feel obligated to talk, and she wasn't sure if that was kindness or surrender.; He didn't fight it. Or if he did, she didn't see it, and the not-seeing was its own kind of evidence. 'It might be good for you, Munch' — six words that would live in her chest like shrappel. He said it while clearing his throat and ordering garlic bread and she would replay it for years, the casualness of it, the way he talked about the end of her world like it was a character-building exercise. His silence about the move was a second abandonment. The first was the spare room. This was the spare room but bigger — the spare country, the spare life, the spare father who let her go without a war. She didn't know yet that his silence might have been grief. She didn't know that men sometimes clear their throats because crying isn't allowed. She only knew he let her leave, and she would never fully forgive that sentence, that 'it might be good for you,' never.; The FaceTime calls became a chore, not because she didn't love him but because loving him through a screen was like eating with your hands tied — you could do it but everything was harder and messier and less satisfying. His voice was still the low rumble but it came with background noise now, pub sounds, Siân sounds, England sounds, and she could hear her old country without being in it and the distance was a physical ache she couldn't name. 'How's it going, Munch?' — 'Fine.' The same exchange every week, shrinking, hollowing out, becoming a performance of connection instead of connection itself. She started dodging the calls, then felt guilty, then dodged them again. The guilt was worse than the dodge because she knew he was trying, she could see him trying, but trying wasn't the same as being there and being there was all she wanted and he wasn't there, he was in a flat in Croydon with a woman she'd never met and a life she wasn't part of, and every FaceTime call reminded her of this, every background laugh was proof that England had continued without her.; The FaceTime calls became shorter and more awkward, not because either of them wanted them to be, but because there was nothing to say. What was she supposed to tell him about? Chess? Czech YouTube? The field she ran in? These weren't things he knew, weren't things he could picture. His questions felt like checklists — 'how's school?' 'you keeping busy?' 'mum doing alright?' — and her answers felt like lies — 'fine,' 'yeah,' 'yeah.' The distance that was already there became geographic and temporal and he was living in a different season, a different lockdown, a different world with Siân in it, and she could hear that world in the background of every call, the sounds of a flat that wasn't hers, the life that continued without her. She started dodging calls. Then feeling guilty. Then dodging them again. The guilt was worse than the dodge because she knew he was trying, she could hear him trying, but trying wasn't the same as being there.; He became the ghost in the room when Pavel was there. Every kindness from Pavel was a reminder that Dad wasn't providing kindnesses anymore — not in person, not in the same way, not with the same ease. The FaceTime calls were shorter now. 'How's school?' 'Fine.' 'Mum doing alright?' 'Yeah.' The rhythm was a skeleton, bones without meat. She didn't tell him about Pavel. She didn't know why. Maybe because saying it would make it real, would mean England was really gone, would mean the flat above Ladbrokes was really someone else's life now. Or maybe because she didn't want him to feel replaced, didn't want to give him that power, didn't want to admit that there was a space that could be filled by someone who wasn't him. The 'Dad' slip lived in her like a secret she was keeping from both of them — from Pavel because he didn't deserve it, from Marcus because he didn't earn it. She didn't know which betrayal was worse.; The visit made the distance geographical in a way that FaceTime hadn't. The flat in Croydon was proof — he'd built a life with furniture and a nursery and a coffee-stain-touching woman and a child with his blue eyes, and the life was real and solid and didn't have a knocking radiator because it wasn't her life, was never going to be her life, was a life she could visit but not live in. He said 'alright, Munch?' and she heard the effort in it, the reaching, and she reached back with 'yeah, fine' and the distance between those two sentences was the distance between Croydon and Benešov, between who he was and who he was trying to be for her. The Christmas lie — 'I'm busy' — was easier than going back to that flat, easier than watching him try to be her dad in a house that proved he was someone else's dad now. She didn't know if she was protecting herself or punishing him. Maybe both.; The calls come once a month now, maybe less. The conversations have skeletons where meat used to be — 'how's school' and 'fine' and 'mum doing alright' and 'yeah' and the silence that isn't comfortable but isn't hostile, just empty. She dodged two calls this spring and felt sick about it for days after each one and still didn't call back. The guilt is worse than the dodge because she knows he's trying, she can hear him trying through the phone, but trying isn't being there and being there is the only thing she wants and he can't give it from Croydon. She's becoming someone he won't recognise. The Czech is faster now. The references are local. The life is here, not there. Every call is proof of the distance, and the proof hurts more than the silence would.

Kl\u00e1ra Liebigov\u00e1.context: family; The anxiousness was there from the first day home — the hovering, the checking, the not-sleeping-because-what-if. An immigrant woman alone with a tiny thing in a country that wasn't hers, no family nearby, Markéta the only Czech person she knew, she'd left everything to be with Marcus and now there was this small angry baby and she was convinced she'd break her. Anna felt it as tension in the arms, the too-tight grip, the breath that caught at every sound. Love and fear looked the same from the inside.; The first small rejection — every English word Anna said was a door closing in Klára's face, and Anna didn't know it, couldn't know it, but Mum heard it as losing, as the English world taking her daughter before she even had her. When Anna answered Czech with English, always English, Klára's face did the thing, and Anna thought Mum was angry but she was grieving. The distance between them started here, in the wrong language, in the words that came out English when they should have come out Czech.; The stone was a crack. Not a break — the break would come later — but a crack. Anna learned that Mum didn't understand what mattered. Mum thought the blue-black stone was rubbish. Mum said 'proboha' when she bumped the fridge like it was the fridge's fault. Mum's love was heavy and anxious and it crushed things sometimes without meaning to. The stone was the first time Anna understood that her mother could hurt her without knowing it.; The car park. The crying. Before nursery, Mum was the anxious arms, the tight face, the wrong tension — but she was there. After the car park, Anna understood that she made Mum cry. Not deliberately. Not with intention. But the fact of her — the fact of being someone who needed to be left somewhere — was a wound she gave her mother every morning. The clinging at drop-off got worse before it got better, and then it got worse again, and Klára started arriving earlier to cry in the car before coming in, and Anna started watching the car park window instead of the fish tank, and neither of them talked about it because Anna didn't have the words and Klára didn't know she'd been seen. The guilt entered the relationship like damp enters a wall — not visible, but structural, always there, weakening things.; The Saturday routine became their small country, the two of them, and it was the only place where the borders were calm. Sainsbury's. The café. The walk home with the magazine. Mum's face was looser on Saturdays. Mum's hands were less tight on hers in the shop. They had a booth and a routine and a thing that was theirs without Dad, without Czech grandmothers on video calls, without the flat and its knocking radiator and its growing damp. This was the peace before. This was the thing Anna would miss most when it broke — not the flat, not the routine, but the one morning a week where Mum wasn't frightened of the world.; The anxious arms got heavier at the exact moment Anna started wanting to pull away, and the physics of that created something new — a tension, a resistance, a rubber band being stretched. Mum's grip was too tight on the walk to school. Mum's face was too tight when Anna spoke English. Mum's love was becoming something that smothered instead of warmed, and Anna couldn't say this because she didn't know it, she just felt it as the instinct to pull her hand away, to sit further from Mum on the sofa, to want the flat to be empty sometimes instead of full of Mum's worry. The guilt of wanting distance from someone who was clearly trying so hard — that was new too.; The anxious arms got sadder. The tight face got tighter. Mum was becoming someone who cried in the bathroom and smoked on the balcony and said 'proboha' more often and bumped the fridge every day and never learned where it was. Anna's love for her mother became complicated — not less, but tangled with the knowledge that Mum was hiding things, that the flat had rooms Mum didn't let her into, that the woman who held her too tight was also the woman who couldn't hold herself together. The wallpaper curl by the bathroom door grew under Anna's fingernail and it was the only proof she had that she'd stood there, that she'd heard, that she'd chosen silence over knocking.; Mum started saying things she shouldn't. Small comments about Dad that she'd take back or apologise for — 'he doesn't appreciate what we have' followed by 'forget I said that, I'm just tired.' Anna learned to hear what Mum meant instead of what Mum said. The bathroom crying became routine, not every night but enough, and Anna memorised the sounds: tap on, sniffle, tap off, silence too long, tap on again. The wallpaper patch grew. The guilt grew too — the guilt of sitting outside listening, the guilt of not knocking, the guilt of being the reason Mum was crying, maybe, possibly, she didn't know. Mum's love was still heavy. Mum's hands were still too tight on the walk to school. But now there was something else — the comments, the taken-back words, the tight mouth when Dad's name came up, and Anna became a translator of silence, a reader of what wasn't said, and seven was too young for that job but she didn't know how to quit.; The dam broke. Mum stopped hiding in the bathroom with the tap running and started crying in the kitchen, the sitting room, the car, like the structure that had been holding her grief back had collapsed with the front door closing. She told Anna things — 'Dad chose his freedom over us,' 'he doesn't care about anyone but himself,' then 'I shouldn't say that, forget it, I'm sorry,' then the same things again a week later. Anna became the container for Mum's anger, the receiver of words that should have been said to a therapist or a friend or a wall, not to an eight-year-old who couldn't close the lid on what she'd heard. The Saturday routine stayed but Mum's face in the Sainsbury's café was looser in a bad way now, not relaxed but emptied, and the magazine felt like a consolation prize instead of a treat.; Mum's grief mutated into information Anna never asked for. 'He was seeing her before, you know.' 'I gave up everything for him — my country, my family, everything.' 'Siân from work. That's where he was on the late shifts.' Each sentence was a brick in a wall Anna hadn't agreed to build. She learned to nod without listening, to make her face go blank, to not ask follow-up questions because questions made Mum cry and Mum crying was worse than Mum talking. The Saturday routine survived but the Sainsbury's café booth felt different now — Mum's face was emptier, the magazine was a prop, and the silence between them was full of things they both knew but wouldn't say. Anna became the container for Mum's anger and she didn't consent to this role but there was no one else to fill it.; The move was Mum's decision and Anna knew it in her bones before Klára confirmed it. Sitting at that kitchen table with the cold tea and the sorry face — Mum wasn't asking, she was telling, and the telling was wrapped in apology but the apology didn't change the telling. Anna started holding the move against her mother like a stone in her pocket, something to touch when she needed to remember who to blame. Every box Mum packed was evidence. Every 'it'll be good for you' was a wound. The gratitude for Babička's house would come later, much later, not yet — now there was only the woman who took England away, and Anna looked at her and saw the enemy, and the enemy was wearing her mother's face.; Closer and more suffocating at the same time. Mum was happier near Babička — her face was looser, she wasn't crying in the bathroom, she said 'proboha' less often. But Anna still couldn't breathe sometimes. Couldn't complain because Mum had done this for her, right? This was supposed to be good. She was supposed to be grateful. The gratitude was a cage with nice bars. She loved her mother and wanted space from her mother and both things were true and neither was allowed to win.; The lockdown pushed them together and the pressure cracked something that hadn't fully healed from the flat above Ladbrokes. Mum's anxiety returned like weather — not the bathroom crying, not yet, but the hovering, the checking, the way she appeared in doorways without knocking, the way her face went tight when Anna spoke English on the phone to Dad. The house was smaller than the flat in Tooting had been, and there was no school to escape to, no track, no café with Tereza, just rooms and rooms and Mum moving through them like she was tracking something. Anna started leaving rooms when Klára entered them. Not dramatically — just getting up, finding somewhere else to be, the field, the garden, the attic with the door pushed shut. She didn't know how to say 'you're smothering me' so she said nothing and left instead, and the leaving became its own language, and the distance between them grew in centimeters that felt like kilometers.; Mum's grief was louder than Anna's — that was the problem. Klára cried on the phone, cried in the kitchen when she visited for the funeral, cried at the graveside, cried in the car on the way back to London, and Anna held all of it because there was no one else to hold it and the holding made her harder, made her the stone again, made her the container for a woman's grief that wasn't hers to carry. The old pattern — the bathroom crying, the 'proboha,' the anxious arms — returned in a new form. 'I should have visited more.' 'I left them here and went to England and now he's gone.' The immigrant guilt poured out and Anna caught it in her eleven-year-old hands and didn't know how to put it down. She loved her mother. She couldn't breathe around her mother. Both things. Still.; Mum's grief returned in full force — the bathroom crying, the 'proboha,' the immigrant guilt about leaving for England, about not being here when he got sick, about choosing Marcus over her family. 'I should have visited more. I left them here and went to England and now he's gone.' The words poured out and Anna caught them in her eleven-year-old hands and didn't know how to put them down. The old pattern — anxious arms, tight face, the container role — reactivated like a switch. She was eleven and holding her mother's grief again because there was no one else to hold it and the holding made her harder, made her the stone, made her the thing that doesn't cry at funerals.; Mum noticed the body before Anna did. The comments started small — 'stand up straight, you're slouching,' 'that top is too tight,' 'boys are going to start looking at you' — and each one was a small invasion, a claim staked on territory that Anna hadn't mapped yet. Klára's anxiety had a new target: Anna's changing body, Anna's potential attractiveness, Anna's vulnerability to the male gaze that Klára understood better than Anna did. The anxious arms became the commenting arms, the critical arms, the arms that reached to pull her shirt down or fix her posture or warn her about things she didn't understand yet. Anna shrank from this attention the way she shrank from all of Mum's worry — by leaving the room, by running, by sitting in Babička's kitchen where nobody commented on bodies, they just put food in front of you and let you eat. The gratitude-cage of living with Mum got smaller. The love was still there. The suffocation was still there. Both things. Still.; The gratitude cage got smaller. Every time Klára defended Pavel, every time she said 'he's trying,' every time she asked Anna to be polite, the bars closed in another inch. Anna knew the script — Mum sacrificed everything, Mum left her country, Mum is doing her best — but knowing the script didn't make her want to perform it. The fights got meaner because the truth got closer. 'You chose this life, I didn't' — that was the blade, and she used it, and she knew she was being unfair, and the unfairness didn't stop her because unfair was the only weapon she had against a woman who had already won by being her mother. The love was still there. The suffocation was still there. But now there was something new — the recognition that Klára had been Anna's age once, had been a girl with a mother who was probably anxious too, had grown up and chosen a man and a country and lost both and was choosing again, and the choosing was brave or stupid or both, and Anna couldn't decide if she respected it or hated it.; The fights got sharper because the weapons got real. When Anna was younger, the arguments were about homework and bedtimes and screen time — the standard ammunition of mothers and daughters. Now they're about identity, about England, about who chose what and who suffered for it. 'I didn't ask you to leave England' — Anna said it and watched it land and watched her mother's face collapse and felt powerful for one sick second before the guilt swallowed the power whole. The gratitude cage is smaller now. Every time Klára defends Pavel, every time she says 'he's trying,' the bars close in. Anna knows she's being unfair. The unfairness doesn't stop her. She's 14 and she's discovered that cruelty is a way to get space from someone who won't give it willingly, and she hates herself for using it, and she uses it anyway.

Marie Novotn\u00e1.context: family; Became the safest person without trying, without deciding, without any conscious effort at all. The kitchen did it. The koláče. The humming. The way she put food in front of Anna and didn't ask how school was, didn't check her face for sadness, didn't say 'proboha' when she bumped into things. Babička's love was the opposite of Mum's — it was light, it was undemanding, it was present without pressure. Anna didn't trust it at first because she'd been trained to expect anxiety from the Czech women in her life, but by November she was seeking it, drifting into the kitchen after school, sitting at the table without speaking, eating whatever appeared, letting the warmth of it fill the hollow that school carved out. She didn't know yet that this relationship would save her. She just knew that Babička's kitchen was the only place in Benešov where she could breathe.; Babička shrank. That's the only word for it — the woman who filled the kitchen with koláče and humming and presence became smaller after the funeral, quieter, as if Děda had been taking up space she didn't know she was leaning on. The kitchen was still the safest place but it was emptier now, and Anna started going there more often, not for the food but for the company, sitting at the table the way Děda used to sit — patient, undemanding, present. She didn't know she was learning patience by watching it leave. She didn't know she was becoming the thing she'd lost.; Babička shrank. That's the only word for it. The woman who filled the kitchen with koláče and humming and presence became smaller after the funeral, quieter, as if Děda had been taking up space she didn't know she was leaning on. The kitchen was still the safest place but it was emptier now, and Anna started going there more often — not for the food but for the company, sitting at the table the way Děda used to sit, patient, undemanding, present. She didn't know she was learning patience by watching it leave. She didn't know she was becoming the thing she'd lost.; Babička became the only room left. The kitchen didn't ask questions. The kitchen didn't defend Pavel. The kitchen just put food down and hummed and let Anna sit in silence for as long as she needed. Anna started going there more — not just after school but before school, during fights, during the evenings when Pavel's car was in the driveway and the house felt like it was shrinking. Babička never said 'you should be nicer to him.' Babička never said 'your mother deserves happiness.' Babička just made koláče and watched her old Czech TV shows and let the world be what it was. This neutrality was the only safe thing and Anna clung to it without admitting she was clinging. She didn't know that Babička had been Klára's age once too, had made choices too, had probably felt the same cage of gratitude and resentment. She didn't know that Babička's silence was a choice, not an absence. She just knew the kitchen was warm and nobody asked her to be anyone she wasn't.; Babička is still the safe place but the safe place is slower now. She notices things she doesn't want to notice — the way Babička's hands shake when she cuts apples, the way she forgets which drawer the tea is in, the way she says Anna's name and then pauses like she's checking she got it right. The catalogue of decline lives in Anna's stomach next to all the other fears. She hates that she's tracking it. She hates that part of her is already preparing for the next loss. Babička's kitchen is still warm and undemanding but the warmth is thinner somehow, like the house is letting in a draft that wasn't there before.

Franti\u0161ek Novotn\u00fd.context: family; He went from the quiet man in the corner who smelled like tobacco to the only calm thing in the house. The chess games did it — not because he was teaching her strategy, but because he was teaching her patience, and patience from a man who moved slowly and spoke rarely was different from patience from a woman who moved quickly and said 'proboha.' Děda never asked how she was feeling. Děda never checked if she was okay. Děda just set up the pieces and said 'tvoje tah' — your turn — and let her take it. She didn't know this was love. She didn't know that some people say 'I love you' by sitting across from you for three hours without needing anything back. She didn't know he was sick. She thought the naps were normal. She thought the slowness was just age. She thought they had time.; He went from present to absent in the span of weeks but the teaching continued without him. She hears 'dobře, dobře' when she makes a good decision. She took the chess set to the attic and put it on the shelf where she can see it from the bed — not to play, she can't play, the thought of moving those pieces without him makes her throat close — but to see. To remember that patience existed. That someone sat with her for hours without needing anything back. The dead don't leave. They just stop being in the room. She didn't know this before. She knows it now.; He went from present to absent in weeks but the teaching continued without him. She hears 'dobře, dobře' when she makes a good move, not his voice, just the shape of his approval, the memory of it. The chess set on the shelf is a gravestone she can see from her bed. She can't play. The thought of moving those pieces without him makes her throat close. But she looks at it every night. She doesn't know if this is grieving or holding on or both. She doesn't know the difference.

Si\u00e2n Liebig.context: family; She became real. Not a person — Anna still hadn't seen her face — but a fact. A force. A name that lived in the flat like mould, growing in the corners of conversations, appearing without warning. 'Your father and Siân.' 'Siân's probably got a nice flat.' 'Siân this, Siân that.' Anna drew her in her head: tall, thin, mean-looking, the kind of woman who took things. The villain in a story that was supposed to be about her family. She didn't know yet that hating someone you've never met is exhausting. She just knew the name made her stomach clench and her hand reach for the skin around her thumbnail.; The villain died in the doorway of the Lebanon Road flat. The tall mean woman who stole Dad — that Siân never existed, was never real, was a story Anna told herself because stories with villains have endings and the truth didn't. The real Siân was short and nervous and touched her coffee stain and laughed at the wrong moments and asked about school in a voice that was trying so hard it made Anna's teeth hurt. You can hate a villain. You can't hate a woman who is clearly terrified of you, clearly trying, clearly aware that she's standing in the middle of someone else's family and doesn't know where to put her hands. Anna didn't forgive her. Forgiveness wasn't the right word. She just couldn't sustain the hatred anymore and the hatred had been useful — it was a clean feeling, a simple feeling, and without it she had to feel the complicated thing underneath which was that Siân wasn't the problem, had never been the problem, the problem was Dad leaving and you can't hate someone for leaving if you also understand why they left and she didn't want to understand but she was starting to.

Poppy Liebig.context: family; The half-sister went from abstract to concrete — blue eyes, grabbed fistfuls of Anna's hair, said 'Anna' in the English toddler way that makes every name sound like a question. She didn't feel sisterhood. She didn't feel jealousy, not exactly. She felt like she was looking at evidence: this is what he does, he builds, he makes people, he leaves them in clean flats without knocking radiators and he visits on Sundays and says 'alright, Munch?' and the world keeps spinning. Poppy was three and didn't know any of this and that was the worst part, the not-knowing, the innocence, the fact that this small blue-eyed person would grow up with the easy version and never know there had been a hard version. Anna looked at her and chose nothing. The nothing cracked later, in the attic room, at 3am, when she imagined Poppy older, Poppy at fourteen, Poppy sitting on someone's shoulders, and the crack let something in that she still can't name.

Elara Chung.context: best friend — secret language, sleepovers, Domino's, the only person who knew about Dad leaving, she moved to Wales in Year 6 and Anna didn't cry but wanted to; She became the escape hatch. The first person outside the flat who felt like shelter, who didn't need explanations, who just let Anna exist without asking her to translate herself. The backwards language was a country nobody else could enter. Elara's flat was bigger and nobody cried in the bathroom and nobody had a spare room with a closed door. Anna told Elara that Dad moved into the spare room — 'he snores too loud' — but not about the crying, not about the pipe, not about the arguments the radiator carried into her bedroom. She kept the flat's secrets inside the flat where they belonged. Elara never pushed. That was the thing. Elara just made space and Anna filled it or didn't, and the not-having-to-explain was the closest thing to peace she'd felt since the slippers moved.; She became the only person outside the flat who knew the shape of the thing, not the details — not about the crying or the radiator or the spare room — but the fact of it, the before and after. The friendship bracelet was a contract written in thread: I know and I'm here and I won't make you explain. Anna didn't have to perform okay-ness with Elara the way she did at home with Mum or at Nando's with Dad. Elara made space and Anna filled it or didn't, and the not-having-to-perform was the only place she could breathe.; The goodbye was too big for two ten-year-olds and they performed it like they understood what 'never seeing each other at school again' meant, but they didn't, not yet. The pizza, the crying, the bracelet — it was all correct, all the right beats, but underneath it was the panic of two kids who had never lost anyone trying to figure out the choreography of loss. The FaceTime calls went from daily to weekly to monthly to a happy birthday text, and the friendship bracelet is still on her wrist, grey now, and the friendship isn't dead but it's not alive either, it's somewhere in between, in the space where daily used to be. The friendship didn't end. It just thinned. Like the thread on the bracelet, like the signal, like all the things you promise to keep when you're crying and the promise feels like the only thing keeping the world together.

Aiden Walsh.context: school friend — played tag at lunch, nothing deep, he moved away and she didn't care

Mat\u011bj Kr\u00e1l.context: first Czech friend — translated the world, played Minecraft for hours, patient when she couldn't find the words, she owes him more than she'd ever say out loud; still close but weird now — he acts different since she grew up, she misses how easy it was, he probably likes her and she doesn't know how to feel about that; He went from a face in the corridor to the most important person she didn't choose. Before Konopiště: nothing. After Konopiště: the translator, the bridge, the person who sat next to her in class and whispered the words she needed. The Minecraft server made it solid — hours on Discord, building a city, her Czech getting faster, his English getting better. He never said 'angličanka' except as a joke between them, and the joke was theirs, not theirs-and-everyone-else's. She trusted him because he didn't expect anything back, but she doesn't know if that trust is real or just gratitude wearing a mask. She owes him more than she'd ever say out loud.; He became the outside world. The Discord calls stretched from an hour to three to six, the Minecraft server becoming a second country where she could build things and destroy things and the language didn't matter as much because they had a shared world that existed outside Benešov, outside Czech, outside the house with the closing walls. She told him things she didn't tell Mum — that she missed English, that the church bells still sounded wrong, that she dreamed about the radiator. He never made it weird. He just kept building and let her talk or not talk, and the not-having-to-perform was the same freedom she'd had with Elara Chung, but this time in Czech, which made it different, which made it feel like the language was becoming hers instead of being imposed on her. She trusted him because he didn't carry the anxiety that every other Czech person in her life carried. He was just a boy on a server, placing blocks, and she could be just a girl placing blocks next to him.; The ease curdled. Not all at once — there was no moment, no conversation, no confession — just the slow accumulation of looks that lasted too long and silences where silences hadn't been before. He started complimenting her in March, small things, 'nice run today' becoming 'you looked fast out there' becoming 'hezká' said to his mum when he thought Anna couldn't hear, and each one landed on her like a insect she wanted to brush off but didn't know how. The Discord calls continued — Minecraft, school complaints, the same rhythm — but now she was aware of her own voice in a way she hadn't been before, checking her words, checking his tone, checking the silence for something she didn't want to name. She missed the simplicity. She missed being just a voice on a server, just blocks and jokes, just Matěj who never looked at her like that. She didn't know how to make it go back and she didn't know if she wanted it to go back and both answers felt wrong so she pretended nothing had changed and the pretending was its own kind of distance.; The awkwardness hardened into architecture — a wall built from too-long looks and soft goodbyes and the way he says 'hezký' about things that aren't hezký, about things she built in Minecraft that are just blocks, just pixels, just the same rectangles they've always been. She logs on less now. The server feels like his territory suddenly, like his feelings have claim over the space they used to share. She misses the old Matěj — the one who translated the world, who placed blocks without breathing funny, who was just a boy and not a boy who liked her. She doesn't know how to grieve a friendship that's still technically alive. She doesn't know if she's allowed to mourn something that hasn't ended.

Viktorie Mal\u00e1.context: sat next to her in art, drew together, friendship faded when Viktorie went to a different school and neither of them tried to keep it going

Tereza B\u00e1rtov\u00e1.context: best friend — former bully turned closest person, they speak in mixed Czech-English, they get free hot chocolate at her mum's cafe, Tereza is the only one who knows about the Ondřej crush; Became the enemy by accident, by one word, by the casual cruelty of children who don't know they're being cruel. 'Angličanka' — the English girl — whispered on the second day, loud enough for the others to hear, and the laugh that followed, and the face attached to the laugh, brown hair, sharp chin, eyes that found Anna's and held them for a beat too long. The toilet incident cemented it. The sound Tereza made — that intake of breath, that bright sharp laugh — it lived in Anna's chest like a splinter. She hated Tereza with a clean hatred, uncomplicated, the hatred of someone who has been marked as different and then been made to feel it. She didn't know this would become the closest friendship she'd ever have. She couldn't know. She only knew that Tereza Bártová was the face that laughed when she was drowning.; The shift began in the canteen, at the table near the bins, in twenty minutes of silence. Before that, Tereza was the laugh in the toilet, the 'angličanka,' the face that found hers and held it too long. After that, Tereza was the girl who knew that silence was better than sorry. Anna started noticing her differently — not as the enemy but as someone who understood something about grief without being told. The foundation was laid. Not friendship yet. Something underneath friendship. The knowledge that this person would sit with you in the worst moment and not make it worse. That's rare. Anna didn't know it was rare. She was eleven. She knows now.; The bathroom rewired everything. Tereza was the laugh in the toilet, the angličanka, the face that found hers and held it too long — and then she was the hand that slid a pad under the door without being asked, without making it weird, without collecting the debt. The canteen silence sealed it. Twenty minutes of not-talking that said more than any apology could have. Anna didn't forgive Tereza — forgiveness felt too clean, too simple, like erasing something that should stay visible. But she let the cruelty and the kindness exist in the same person, and that was new, that was adult, that was the first time she understood that people could be more than one thing. The café on Náměstí became their country — the back corner, the free hot chocolate from Tereza's mum, the mixed Czech-English that was theirs and no one else's. Tereza never mentioned the bathroom again. Anna never mentioned the angličanka. The past existed between them like a scar — visible, permanent, but no longer bleeding.; From closest friend to ride-or-die, which is a distinction that sounds the same but isn't. The Ondřej thing sealed it. Tereza said no to a boy she might have said yes to because Anna liked him, and that kind of loyalty doesn't come with instructions for gratitude, it just sits there, real and heavy, changing the weight of everything. They started speaking in the mix more — Czech and English tangled together, codeswitching mid-sentence, creating a language that was theirs and no one else's. 'He's such a dick, ale vlastně ne, he's just oblivious, like properly oblivious, úplně mimo.' Anna told Tereza about the hair-flip and Tereza laughed so hard she choked on her hot chocolate and then said 'that's the worst thing I've ever heard' with such genuine admiration that Anna almost felt okay about it. Almost. The friendship had a scar now — the angličanka, the bathroom — and the scar was visible and permanent but the tissue around it was stronger than before, and the Ondřej situation was the thing that proved it.; The voice note could have broken it. 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' — the most honest thing Anna had said about their friendship and it was broadcast to seven people who weren't Tereza.
