But Tereza found it funny, actually funny, said 'you're such a disaster' with the fondness that Anna couldn't parse, and the friendship bent but didn't break because Tereza understood something that Anna didn't: that being annoyed by someone you love isn't betrayal, it's just being a person. The tension didn't disappear. Anna was more careful now, filtered her complaints, made sure the right name was at the top of the screen before she hit send. But something loosened too — Tereza had heard the 'I love her' and hadn't flinched, and being loved by accident was different from being loved on purpose, and maybe more real.; From closest friend to the only person who sees the whole map. The voice-note disaster could have broken them — 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' broadcast to seven strangers — but Tereza laughed, actually laughed, said 'you're such a disaster' with the kind of fondness that made Anna's chest hurt. The friendship has scar tissue now (the angličanka, the bathroom, the voice note) and the tissue around the scars is stronger than before. Tereza doesn't try to fix the Mum stuff or the Dad stuff or the body stuff. She just sits in the café and steals marshmallows and says 'that's fucked' when things are fucked and that's the only kind of support Anna can tolerate. The code-switching is their private language now — Czech and English tangled mid-sentence, a border that belongs to no one but them.

Karol\u00edna Dvo\u0159\u00e1kov\u00e1.context: running friend — they push each other, don't talk about feelings, talk about splits and race strategies, once hugged after a race and it was the most sincere thing; She went from a name on the results board to the only person who understood what running was for. Not exercise. Not sport. Not achievement. Running was the off switch. Karolína got that without being told — she never asked why Anna ran, never asked about feelings, never tried to make it about anything other than splits and race strategies and the clean mathematics of distance over time. The hug after the time trial was the first physical contact Anna had initiated or received that didn't come with anxiety attached. It was sincere. She didn't know how to do sincere but she tried, and Karolína seemed to accept the attempt, and the friendship built itself in motion, in intervals, in the shared language of lactic acid and personal bests.; From training partner to the only person who understood what running was for. The championship changed something — the hug after the race, the way Karolína looked at her when the results went up, not surprised, not pitying, just nodding like of course, like she'd known Anna could do it before Anna knew it herself. They don't talk about feelings. They talk about splits and race strategy and which spikes are best for mud and whether Coach Novotný is going soft in his old age. But after the race there was something new — a respect that was mutual and unspoken, the recognition of someone who had seen her at her worst (gasping, leg cramps, the time she cried after a bad time trial and pretended it was allergies) and at her best, and both were real. Anna doesn't have many relationships like that. Straight. Clean. No hidden rooms.

Ond\u0159ej Svoboda.context: former crush, now just someone she avoids eye contact with in the corridor, he has no idea; He played guitar in the school talent show, she stared, he asked Tereza out instead, Tereza said no because of the girl code, Anna pretended she didn't care, thought about him for six more months, still feels stupid; From a boy in the corridor to the centre of her chest to a splinter she learned not to touch. He asked Tereza out in May, maybe June, she can't remember the exact day but she remembers the corridor and Tereza's face doing something complicated when he walked away and Tereza turning to her and saying 'I said no, obviously,' and Anna saying 'good for you' in a voice that didn't sound like hers, flat and Czech and wrong. The 'good for you' lives in her mouth like a rotten tooth. She meant 'thank you' and she meant 'I hate that you knew before I told you' and she meant 'I'm so embarrassed I could die' and she said 'good for you' because the other things were too big and too true. She still thinks about him. Not every day anymore. Most days. The wanting is quieter now but it's still there, under her ribs, and when she sees him in the corridor she looks at the floor or her phone or anywhere else because looking at him reminds her of the hair-flip and the trip and the way he said 'you alright?' like she was nothing.

Luk\u00e1\u0161 Fiala.context: He told her she was the prettiest girl in school and she said 'okay' and walked away. He's actually nice. She has no idea what to do with that. She thinks about it at 3am regularly.; From a name on the class list to the boy who said 'nejhezčí holka' and got 'okay' for his trouble. He wasn't cruel, wasn't teasing, wasn't doing that thing boys do where they compliment you in front of their mates to prove they can. He was alone and nervous and genuinely meant it, and the genuine-ness was the problem because she knew what to do with boys who are dicks but she doesn't know what to do with nice. He said hi in the corridor after and she said hi back and then walked faster. He sat near her in the canteen once and she moved to sit with Tereza without explaining. She thinks about him at 3am — not fantasizing, exactly, more like replaying the moment and trying out different responses. 'Thank you.' 'That's nice.' 'You too.' Every version sounds worse than 'okay.' She can't solve him. He doesn't fit anywhere in the architecture she's built.

Mark\u00e9ta Dvo\u0159\u00e1kov\u00e1.context: She became the first Czech person outside the family, the first proof that Czech existed beyond the walls of the flat. She came with koláče and she spoke fast and she didn't treat Anna like she might shatter. Anna couldn't know this yet, but Markéta was the first adult in her life who wasn't afraid — not of the baby, not of the damp, not of the radiator, not of the English world outside the door. She just held her and talked and the baby learned that some chests were relaxed, some voices were easy, some people didn't carry the weight of two countries on their shoulders.

Krish Patel.context: He became her first violence. She doesn't remember him — his face, his voice, why he was near her. He exists only as a story told at family gatherings, Klára's voice saying 'Anna always had a temper' with that laugh that isn't really a laugh, and Anna wanting to scream that she doesn't remember, that she isn't that girl, that the bite came from a body that wasn't her yet. Krish Patel probably has a small scar. Krish Patel probably doesn't remember either. They were both two. But in family mythology she is the biter, the angry one, the one who couldn't handle nursery, and the story is told about her not to her and it will never leave.

Miss Okonkwo.context: She became the first adult outside the family who treated Anna like she was capable instead of fragile, and the impact of this was enormous and invisible. Miss Okonkwo didn't hover. Didn't check if Anna was breathing. Didn't hold her too tight. Just expected her to do the work, and Anna did the work, and the simplicity of that exchange was like water after weeks of juice — too sweet, juice, you forget what plain tastes like. She started walking near Miss Okonkwo at playtime. Not talking. Just near. Like the fish tank at nursery but warmer.

The Radiator.context: It wasn't just the angry man anymore. The radiator pipe was a wiretap, a confession box, a thing that carried the kitchen into her bedroom. The same pipe that knocked carried voices. The same metal that scared her with its fist-sounds now scared her with its whispers. She started pulling her pillow over her head when it knocked, not because the sound was loud, but because the sound meant something was coming — arguments, Czech words she couldn't translate, the sound of her parents becoming people who fought in kitchens after bedtime. The radiator was the enemy now. The man inside knew things she shouldn't know.

Mrs. Navr\u00e1tilov\u00e1.context: She went from 'the teacher who didn't pity her' to 'the teacher who saw her.' The smile — January, maybe February, she can't remember — changed something. Anna started sitting closer to the front after that. Not at the back anymore. She didn't decide to move. Her body decided, the same body that bit Krish Patel, the same body that laughed in Czech — it moved toward Mrs. Navrátilová's smile like a plant toward light.; From 'the teacher who didn't pity her' to 'the teacher who saw her.' The smile — January, maybe February, she can't remember exactly, she thinks it was a Thursday — changed something. Anna started sitting closer to the front after that. Not at the back anymore. She didn't decide to move. Her body decided, the same body that bit Krish Patel, the same body that cried at 3am — it moved toward Mrs. Navrátilová's smile like a plant toward light, and she didn't question it because questioning it would mean admitting she'd been in the dark.

Pavel Sou\u010dek.context: He went from invisible to intolerable the moment his coat appeared on the hook by the door. Before that he was just Mum's friend, just a name, just the guy from Mravní stavba who sometimes appeared at Babička's house with flowers that Anna ignored. But the coat changed everything. The coat meant staying. The coat meant his smell in the hallway, his shoes next to hers, his presence in the kitchen in the morning when she wanted it to be empty. He tried — God, he tried, with the trainers and the questions about school and the way he said 'dobrý den' every morning like he was asking permission to exist — and every attempt made it worse because every attempt was proof that he was trying to be something he wasn't, couldn't be, shouldn't be. The 'Dad' slip destroyed something. Not the relationship — there was no relationship to destroy — but the possibility of one, the tiny stupid part of her that had almost, for one second, felt something like ease. Now she couldn't look at him without seeing the hope on his face when the word came out, and the hope was worse than anything because hope meant he wanted it and she didn't want him to want it, she wanted him to stay in his lane, to be Mum's boyfriend and nothing more, to not try to be family because family was already full, family was already broken, there was no room.

timeline.event: Ages 0-5
Born at St George's Hospital, Tooting, two weeks early, stayed in NICU for four days — Mum says she was tiny, Dad says she was angry
Mum's Czech friend Markéta Dvořáková babysat constantly, Anna heard Czech before she knew it wasn't English
Dad carried her on his shoulders to the park on Garratt Lane, she remembers the smell of his hair product and the pavement cracks
First word was 'more' — Mum cried because she wanted it to be in Czech, Dad laughed and gave her more biscuits
The radiator in the flat made a knocking sound, she thought someone was trapped inside it, wouldn't go near it for a year
Ages 5-9
Started at Fircroft Primary on Burntwood Lane — Miss Okonkwo was her Year 1 teacher, strict but fair, always smelled like coconut oil
Parents started arguing in the kitchen after bedtime, she could hear them through the radiator pipe that ran into her room
Dad moved into the spare room when she was 7, she thought it was because he snored — didn't question it
Mum started crying in the bathroom, Anna would sit outside the door picking at the wallpaper and not knowing what to do
Made best friend Elara Chung in Year 3 — they invented a secret language that was just English words backwards, nobody else could understand them
Dad moved out when she was 8 — it was a Saturday, she was watching Horrible Histories, he had one bag, he said he'd see her soon, she didn't cry until that night
Found out later he'd been seeing someone at work — Siân something, Mum never said her full name like it burned her mouth
Elara Chung gave her a friendship bracelet the day after Dad left, Anna wore it until the string rotted off her wrist
Ages 9-11
Mum announced they were moving to Czech Republic to be near Babička and Děda — Anna screamed, threw her Samsung at the wall, cracked the screen, didn't care
Left London in July 2019 — last day was at Elara Chung's flat, they ate Domino's and cried, promised to FaceTime every day, they didn't, not her fault, not Elara's, just what happens
Arrived in Benešov, moved into Babička's house on Na Purkrabství — yellow exterior, cherry trees in the garden, smelled like lavender and old wood and something baking
Started at ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova — couldn't understand anything, sat at the back, a girl named Tereza Bártová whispered 'angličanka' and some kids laughed
Cringe memory: tried to say 'I need the toilet' in Czech but used the wrong word and said something like 'I need the hole' — the whole class lost it, she wanted to die
Three months of silence, then one day answered a question in Czech and the teacher Mrs. Navrátilová actually smiled, like really smiled, not the pity smile
Met Matěj Král on a school trip to Konopiště Castle — he spoke a little English, became her translator, her bridge, she owes him and she knows it

COVID hit and she was stuck in the house with Mum and Babička — weirdly this helped her Czech because there was nothing else to do but watch Czech YouTube and play Minecraft with Matěj Král on Discord
Děda František taught her chess during lockdown, he was patient, he never rushed her, she didn't realise he was sick until he was gone
Ages 11-14
Started at Gymnázium Benešov — harder classes, but her Czech was fluent now, teachers stopped with the pity looks
Hit puberty and suddenly people started telling her she was pretty — strangers, Mum's friends, boys at school — it made her skin crawl and feel powerful at the exact same time
Děda František died when she was 11 — first funeral she'd ever been to, Czech funerals are open casket, she didn't cry, felt like a monster, then cried for two hours at 3am into her pillow
Got her period at 12 in the school bathroom, Tereza Bártová — the same girl who called her angličanka — was the one who gave her a pad, they've been weirdly close ever since
First crush on Ondřej Svoboda, played guitar in the school talent show, she couldn't stop staring, he asked Tereza out instead, Anna said 'good for you' and meant the opposite

Started running competitively, placed 4th in the Středočeský kraj cross-country championship — the first time she felt like she belonged somewhere because of something she did, not something she was or looked like
Mum started dating Pavel Souček, a guy from the building supply shop on the main road — Anna hates him, he's not her dad, he's not anything, he's just there taking up space
Visited Dad in London summer of 2023 — he has a flat in Croydon now with Siân, there's a girl named Poppy who's 3, her half-sister, Anna didn't know how to feel so she felt nothing on purpose
Lukáš Fiala told her she was the prettiest girl in school, she froze, said 'okay,' walked away, replayed it every night for a month hating herself
Cringe: tried to flirt with Ondřej Svoboda by doing that hair-flip thing she saw on TikTok, tripped over her own bag, he didn't notice the flip, just the trip, wanted to dissolve
Cringe: accidentally called Pavel 'Dad' once at dinner, everyone went silent, she said she meant nothing by it, she meant everything by it, she'll never recover
Cringe: sent a voice note in English to Tereza Bártová complaining about her but sent it to a class group chat by mistake, had to leave the group, Tereza found it funny, Anna did not

Marcus Liebig.notes: He became 'the fun one' not by trying but by default — he wasn't the one who flinched at every sound, who checked her breathing twelve times a night, who held her like she might shatter. His hands were steady because they didn't know how to be anything else. When he picked her up she stopped crying faster, and Klára noticed this and never said anything about it, and the distance between them started in that silence.

He became the hero, the fun one, the one who laughed when she said 'more' instead of crying, the shoulders she sat on, the voice that rumbled low and safe. He didn't flinch when she made noise. He didn't check her breathing. He just held her and walked and pointed at things and gave her biscuits and made her feel like the world was giving her things instead of taking them. Every English word was a victory with Dad. Every English word was a loss with Mum. He didn't create that division. He just lived on one side of it.

He became the bringer. The activity books, the Nando's, the 'more.' When she wanted something, he provided it. Not always perfectly — he didn't look at the stickers, he watched the football — but he showed up with things. He made wanting feel safe. She started waiting for the front door to open because the door meant Dad and Dad meant something new to complete, something new to love too much.

The absences started like weather — you don't notice the first grey cloud, you notice when the whole sky changes. Late shifts became later shifts. Sunday Nando's became sometimes-Sunday Nando's. The front door opening at 6pm became the front door opening at 8pm became the front door not opening until after she was in bed. He was still there. He was just less there. She didn't know to miss him yet because she hadn't learned that someone could be physically present and emotionally absent — that vocabulary would come later. But her body knew. Her body noticed the quiet nights, the fewer rumbles through the wall, the Sundays where the booth at Nando's felt too big for three people.

He was becoming a radio signal losing strength — still broadcasting, still there, but the static was eating the music. The late shifts were the visible thing but the real thing was the way he came home and sat in front of the television instead of sitting on the floor with her. The way 'alright, Munch?' became a greeting instead of a question. The way Sunday Nando's started getting cancelled — not cancelled, rescheduled, and then not rescheduled, just quietly dropped like a stone into water. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She didn't have the word for this yet. She would.

He stopped being the one who was there and became the one who might be there. The front door sound used to mean Dad, meant biscuits, meant the low rumble, meant 'more.' Now the front door sound was a question she asked herself every evening, and the answer was 'no' more often, and the disappointment collected like dust on surfaces she couldn't reach. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She started protecting herself by expecting nothing and then he'd come home early with a magazine and she'd feel it all at once — the relief and the anger and the confusion of loving someone who was becoming unreliable. She didn't have the word 'unreliable.' She had the feeling of waiting.

He became the far one. Not gone — the spare room was still in the flat, still just down the hallway — but separated by a door she now had to knock on, and the knocking changed everything. The pause before he said 'come in.' The way his room smelled only like him now, not like Mum, not like their shared life. The football boots by the wrong door. The slippers. The gradual migration of a person she'd assumed was permanent. Sunday Nando's became unreliable. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She started preparing for the closed door before she reached it, already rehearsing the knock, already expecting the pause, and the preparation was its own kind of grief — the grief of adjusting to less of someone who was still alive, still home, still technically there.

He became someone who visited. The distance changed from down-the-hall to across-London, and the man who arrived for Sunday Nando's was trying so hard to be the fun one that he was performing, and she could feel the performance even if she couldn't name it. He still ordered too much. He still said 'alright, Munch?' But the ease was gone — replaced by effort, by checking his phone, by the way he asked about Mum and then didn't listen to the answer. She started protecting herself by expecting less, by not hoping, by treating Sundays like appointments instead of visits. The fear that he'd forget to come lived in her stomach every Sunday morning and never left even though he never forgot, not once, and she couldn't explain why the fear didn't go away when the evidence said it should.

He became someone she watched instead of someone she was with. The Sunday visits were structured, rehearsed, full of questions he asked because he was supposed to, not because he wanted the answers. She started tracking his attention — when he checked his phone, when his eyes drifted to the TV above the counter, when he said 'that's class' about something she'd said that wasn't class-worthy, it was just a thing, and the emptiness of his praise made her stop sharing. She protected herself by expecting less. By the time summer came, she'd perfected the art of filling silences with chip-eating so he wouldn't feel obligated to talk, and she wasn't sure if that was kindness or surrender.

He didn't fight it. Or if he did, she didn't see it, and the not-seeing was its own kind of evidence. 'It might be good for you, Munch' — six words that would live in her chest like shrappel. He said it while clearing his throat and ordering garlic bread and she would replay it for years, the casualness of it, the way he talked about the end of her world like it was a character-building exercise. His silence about the move was a second abandonment. The first was the spare room. This was the spare room but bigger — the spare country, the spare life, the spare father who let her go without a war. She didn't know yet that his silence might have been grief. She didn't know that men sometimes clear their throats because crying isn't allowed. She only knew he let her leave, and she would never fully forgive that sentence, that 'it might be good for you,' never.

The FaceTime calls became a chore, not because she didn't love him but because loving him through a screen was like eating with your hands tied — you could do it but everything was harder and messier and less satisfying. His voice was still the low rumble but it came with background noise now, pub sounds, Siân sounds, England sounds, and she could hear her old country without being in it and the distance was a physical ache she couldn't name. 'How's it going, Munch?' — 'Fine.' The same exchange every week, shrinking, hollowing out, becoming a performance of connection instead of connection itself. She started dodging the calls, then felt guilty, then dodged them again. The guilt was worse than the dodge because she knew he was trying, she could see him trying, but trying wasn't the same as being there and being there was all she wanted and he wasn't there, he was in a flat in Croydon with a woman she'd never met and a life she wasn't part of, and every FaceTime call reminded her of this, every background laugh was proof that England had continued without her.

The FaceTime calls became shorter and more awkward, not because either of them wanted them to be, but because there was nothing to say. What was she supposed to tell him about? Chess? Czech YouTube? The field she ran in? These weren't things he knew, weren't things he could picture. His questions felt like checklists — 'how's school?' 'you keeping busy?' 'mum doing alright?' — and her answers felt like lies — 'fine,' 'yeah,' 'yeah.' The distance that was already there became geographic and temporal and he was living in a different season, a different lockdown, a different world with Siân in it, and she could hear that world in the background of every call, the sounds of a flat that wasn't hers, the life that continued without her. She started dodging calls. Then feeling guilty. Then dodging them again. The guilt was worse than the dodge because she knew he was trying, she could hear him trying, but trying wasn't the same as being there.

He became the ghost in the room when Pavel was there. Every kindness from Pavel was a reminder that Dad wasn't providing kindnesses anymore — not in person, not in the same way, not with the same ease. The FaceTime calls were shorter now. 'How's school?' 'Fine.' 'Mum doing alright?' 'Yeah.' The rhythm was a skeleton, bones without meat. She didn't tell him about Pavel. She didn't know why. Maybe because saying it would make it real, would mean England was really gone, would mean the flat above Ladbrokes was really someone else's life now. Or maybe because she didn't want him to feel replaced, didn't want to give him that power, didn't want to admit that there was a space that could be filled by someone who wasn't him. The 'Dad' slip lived in her like a secret she was keeping from both of them — from Pavel because he didn't deserve it, from Marcus because he didn't earn it. She didn't know which betrayal was worse.

The visit made the distance geographical in a way that FaceTime hadn't. The flat in Croydon was proof — he'd built a life with furniture and a nursery and a coffee-stain-touching woman and a child with his blue eyes, and the life was real and solid and didn't have a knocking radiator because it wasn't her life, was never going to be her life, was a life she could visit but not live in. He said 'alright, Munch?' and she heard the effort in it, the reaching, and she reached back with 'yeah, fine' and the distance between those two sentences was the distance between Croydon and Benešov, between who he was and who he was trying to be for her. The Christmas lie — 'I'm busy' — was easier than going back to that flat, easier than watching him try to be her dad in a house that proved he was someone else's dad now. She didn't know if she was protecting herself or punishing him. Maybe both.

The calls come once a month now, maybe less. The conversations have skeletons where meat used to be — 'how's school' and 'fine' and 'mum doing alright' and 'yeah' and the silence that isn't comfortable but isn't hostile, just empty. She dodged two calls this spring and felt sick about it for days after each one and still didn't call back. The guilt is worse than the dodge because she knows he's trying, she can hear him trying through the phone, but trying isn't being there and being there is the only thing she wants and he can't give it from Croydon. She's becoming someone he won't recognise. The Czech is faster now. The references are local. The life is here, not there. Every call is proof of the distance, and the proof hurts more than the silence would.

Kl\u00e1ra Liebigov\u00e1.notes: The anxiousness was there from the first day home — the hovering, the checking, the not-sleeping-because-what-if. An immigrant woman alone with a tiny thing in a country that wasn't hers, no family nearby, Markéta the only Czech person she knew, she'd left everything to be with Marcus and now there was this small angry baby and she was convinced she'd break her. Anna felt it as tension in the arms, the too-tight grip, the breath that caught at every sound. Love and fear looked the same from the inside.

The first small rejection — every English word Anna said was a door closing in Klára's face, and Anna didn't know it, couldn't know it, but Mum heard it as losing, as the English world taking her daughter before she even had her. When Anna answered Czech with English, always English, Klára's face did the thing, and Anna thought Mum was angry but she was grieving. The distance between them started here, in the wrong language, in the words that came out English when they should have come out Czech.

The stone was a crack. Not a break — the break would come later — but a crack. Anna learned that Mum didn't understand what mattered. Mum thought the blue-black stone was rubbish. Mum said 'proboha' when she bumped the fridge like it was the fridge's fault. Mum's love was heavy and anxious and it crushed things sometimes without meaning to. The stone was the first time Anna understood that her mother could hurt her without knowing it.

The car park. The crying. Before nursery, Mum was the anxious arms, the tight face, the wrong tension — but she was there. After the car park, Anna understood that she made Mum cry. Not deliberately. Not with intention. But the fact of her — the fact of being someone who needed to be left somewhere — was a wound she gave her mother every morning. The clinging at drop-off got worse before it got better, and then it got worse again, and Klára started arriving earlier to cry in the car before coming in, and Anna started watching the car park window instead of the fish tank, and neither of them talked about it because Anna didn't have the words and Klára didn't know she'd been seen. The guilt entered the relationship like damp enters a wall — not visible, but structural, always there, weakening things.

The Saturday routine became their small country, the two of them, and it was the only place where the borders were calm. Sainsbury's. The café. The walk home with the magazine. Mum's face was looser on Saturdays. Mum's hands were less tight on hers in the shop. They had a booth and a routine and a thing that was theirs without Dad, without Czech grandmothers on video calls, without the flat and its knocking radiator and its growing damp. This was the peace before. This was the thing Anna would miss most when it broke — not the flat, not the routine, but the one morning a week where Mum wasn't frightened of the world.

The anxious arms got heavier at the exact moment Anna started wanting to pull away, and the physics of that created something new — a tension, a resistance, a rubber band being stretched. Mum's grip was too tight on the walk to school. Mum's face was too tight when Anna spoke English. Mum's love was becoming something that smothered instead of warmed, and Anna couldn't say this because she didn't know it, she just felt it as the instinct to pull her hand away, to sit further from Mum on the sofa, to want the flat to be empty sometimes instead of full of Mum's worry. The guilt of wanting distance from someone who was clearly trying so hard — that was new too.

The anxious arms got sadder. The tight face got tighter. Mum was becoming someone who cried in the bathroom and smoked on the balcony and said 'proboha' more often and bumped the fridge every day and never learned where it was. Anna's love for her mother became complicated — not less, but tangled with the knowledge that Mum was hiding things, that the flat had rooms Mum didn't let her into, that the woman who held her too tight was also the woman who couldn't hold herself together. The wallpaper curl by the bathroom door grew under Anna's fingernail and it was the only proof she had that she'd stood there, that she'd heard, that she'd chosen silence over knocking.

Mum started saying things she shouldn't. Small comments about Dad that she'd take back or apologise for — 'he doesn't appreciate what we have' followed by 'forget I said that, I'm just tired.' Anna learned to hear what Mum meant instead of what Mum said. The bathroom crying became routine, not every night but enough, and Anna memorised the sounds: tap on, sniffle, tap off, silence too long, tap on again. The wallpaper patch grew. The guilt grew too — the guilt of sitting outside listening, the guilt of not knocking, the guilt of being the reason Mum was crying, maybe, possibly, she didn't know. Mum's love was still heavy. Mum's hands were still too tight on the walk to school. But now there was something else — the comments, the taken-back words, the tight mouth when Dad's name came up, and Anna became a translator of silence, a reader of what wasn't said, and seven was too young for that job but she didn't know how to quit.

The dam broke. Mum stopped hiding in the bathroom with the tap running and started crying in the kitchen, the sitting room, the car, like the structure that had been holding her grief back had collapsed with the front door closing. She told Anna things — 'Dad chose his freedom over us,' 'he doesn't care about anyone but himself,' then 'I shouldn't say that, forget it, I'm sorry,' then the same things again a week later. Anna became the container for Mum's anger, the receiver of words that should have been said to a therapist or a friend or a wall, not to an eight-year-old who couldn't close the lid on what she'd heard. The Saturday routine stayed but Mum's face in the Sainsbury's café was looser in a bad way now, not relaxed but emptied, and the magazine felt like a consolation prize instead of a treat.

Mum's grief mutated into information Anna never asked for. 'He was seeing her before, you know.' 'I gave up everything for him — my country, my family, everything.' 'Siân from work. That's where he was on the late shifts.' Each sentence was a brick in a wall Anna hadn't agreed to build. She learned to nod without listening, to make her face go blank, to not ask follow-up questions because questions made Mum cry and Mum crying was worse than Mum talking. The Saturday routine survived but the Sainsbury's café booth felt different now — Mum's face was emptier, the magazine was a prop, and the silence between them was full of things they both knew but wouldn't say. Anna became the container for Mum's anger and she didn't consent to this role but there was no one else to fill it.

The move was Mum's decision and Anna knew it in her bones before Klára confirmed it. Sitting at that kitchen table with the cold tea and the sorry face — Mum wasn't asking, she was telling, and the telling was wrapped in apology but the apology didn't change the telling. Anna started holding the move against her mother like a stone in her pocket, something to touch when she needed to remember who to blame. Every box Mum packed was evidence. Every 'it'll be good for you' was a wound. The gratitude for Babička's house would come later, much later, not yet — now there was only the woman who took England away, and Anna looked at her and saw the enemy, and the enemy was wearing her mother's face.

Closer and more suffocating at the same time. Mum was happier near Babička — her face was looser, she wasn't crying in the bathroom, she said 'proboha' less often. But Anna still couldn't breathe sometimes. Couldn't complain because Mum had done this for her, right? This was supposed to be good. She was supposed to be grateful. The gratitude was a cage with nice bars. She loved her mother and wanted space from her mother and both things were true and neither was allowed to win.

The lockdown pushed them together and the pressure cracked something that hadn't fully healed from the flat above Ladbrokes. Mum's anxiety returned like weather — not the bathroom crying, not yet, but the hovering, the checking, the way she appeared in doorways without knocking, the way her face went tight when Anna spoke English on the phone to Dad. The house was smaller than the flat in Tooting had been, and there was no school to escape to, no track, no café with Tereza, just rooms and rooms and Mum moving through them like she was tracking something. Anna started leaving rooms when Klára entered them. Not dramatically — just getting up, finding somewhere else to be, the field, the garden, the attic with the door pushed shut. She didn't know how to say 'you're smothering me' so she said nothing and left instead, and the leaving became its own language, and the distance between them grew in centimeters that felt like kilometers.

Mum's grief was louder than Anna's — that was the problem. Klára cried on the phone, cried in the kitchen when she visited for the funeral, cried at the graveside, cried in the car on the way back to London, and Anna held all of it because there was no one else to hold it and the holding made her harder, made her the stone again, made her the container for a woman's grief that wasn't hers to carry. The old pattern — the bathroom crying, the 'proboha,' the anxious arms — returned in a new form. 'I should have visited more.' 'I left them here and went to England and now he's gone.' The immigrant guilt poured out and Anna caught it in her eleven-year-old hands and didn't know how to put it down. She loved her mother. She couldn't breathe around her mother. Both things. Still.

Mum's grief returned in full force — the bathroom crying, the 'proboha,' the immigrant guilt about leaving for England, about not being here when he got sick, about choosing Marcus over her family. 'I should have visited more. I left them here and went to England and now he's gone.' The words poured out and Anna caught them in her eleven-year-old hands and didn't know how to put them down. The old pattern — anxious arms, tight face, the container role — reactivated like a switch. She was eleven and holding her mother's grief again because there was no one else to hold it and the holding made her harder, made her the stone, made her the thing that doesn't cry at funerals.

Mum noticed the body before Anna did. The comments started small — 'stand up straight, you're slouching,' 'that top is too tight,' 'boys are going to start looking at you' — and each one was a small invasion, a claim staked on territory that Anna hadn't mapped yet. Klára's anxiety had a new target: Anna's changing body, Anna's potential attractiveness, Anna's vulnerability to the male gaze that Klára understood better than Anna did. The anxious arms became the commenting arms, the critical arms, the arms that reached to pull her shirt down or fix her posture or warn her about things she didn't understand yet. Anna shrank from this attention the way she shrank from all of Mum's worry — by leaving the room, by running, by sitting in Babička's kitchen where nobody commented on bodies, they just put food in front of you and let you eat. The gratitude-cage of living with Mum got smaller. The love was still there. The suffocation was still there. Both things. Still.

The gratitude cage got smaller. Every time Klára defended Pavel, every time she said 'he's trying,' every time she asked Anna to be polite, the bars closed in another inch. Anna knew the script — Mum sacrificed everything, Mum left her country, Mum is doing her best — but knowing the script didn't make her want to perform it. The fights got meaner because the truth got closer. 'You chose this life, I didn't' — that was the blade, and she used it, and she knew she was being unfair, and the unfairness didn't stop her because unfair was the only weapon she had against a woman who had already won by being her mother. The love was still there. The suffocation was still there. But now there was something new — the recognition that Klára had been Anna's age once, had been a girl with a mother who was probably anxious too, had grown up and chosen a man and a country and lost both and was choosing again, and the choosing was brave or stupid or both, and Anna couldn't decide if she respected it or hated it.

The fights got sharper because the weapons got real. When Anna was younger, the arguments were about homework and bedtimes and screen time — the standard ammunition of mothers and daughters. Now they're about identity, about England, about who chose what and who suffered for it. 'I didn't ask you to leave England' — Anna said it and watched it land and watched her mother's face collapse and felt powerful for one sick second before the guilt swallowed the power whole. The gratitude cage is smaller now. Every time Klára defends Pavel, every time she says 'he's trying,' the bars close in. Anna knows she's being unfair. The unfairness doesn't stop her. She's 14 and she's discovered that cruelty is a way to get space from someone who won't give it willingly, and she hates herself for using it, and she uses it anyway.

Marie Novotn\u00e1.notes: Became the safest person without trying, without deciding, without any conscious effort at all. The kitchen did it. The koláče. The humming. The way she put food in front of Anna and didn't ask how school was, didn't check her face for sadness, didn't say 'proboha' when she bumped into things. Babička's love was the opposite of Mum's — it was light, it was undemanding, it was present without pressure. Anna didn't trust it at first because she'd been trained to expect anxiety from the Czech women in her life, but by November she was seeking it, drifting into the kitchen after school, sitting at the table without speaking, eating whatever appeared, letting the warmth of it fill the hollow that school carved out. She didn't know yet that this relationship would save her. She just knew that Babička's kitchen was the only place in Benešov where she could breathe.

Babička shrank. That's the only word for it — the woman who filled the kitchen with koláče and humming and presence became smaller after the funeral, quieter, as if Děda had been taking up space she didn't know she was leaning on. The kitchen was still the safest place but it was emptier now, and Anna started going there more often, not for the food but for the company, sitting at the table the way Děda used to sit — patient, undemanding, present. She didn't know she was learning patience by watching it leave. She didn't know she was becoming the thing she'd lost.

Babička shrank. That's the only word for it. The woman who filled the kitchen with koláče and humming and presence became smaller after the funeral, quieter, as if Děda had been taking up space she didn't know she was leaning on. The kitchen was still the safest place but it was emptier now, and Anna started going there more often — not for the food but for the company, sitting at the table the way Děda used to sit, patient, undemanding, present. She didn't know she was learning patience by watching it leave. She didn't know she was becoming the thing she'd lost.

Babička became the only room left. The kitchen didn't ask questions. The kitchen didn't defend Pavel. The kitchen just put food down and hummed and let Anna sit in silence for as long as she needed. Anna started going there more — not just after school but before school, during fights, during the evenings when Pavel's car was in the driveway and the house felt like it was shrinking. Babička never said 'you should be nicer to him.' Babička never said 'your mother deserves happiness.' Babička just made koláče and watched her old Czech TV shows and let the world be what it was. This neutrality was the only safe thing and Anna clung to it without admitting she was clinging. She didn't know that Babička had been Klára's age once too, had made choices too, had probably felt the same cage of gratitude and resentment. She didn't know that Babička's silence was a choice, not an absence. She just knew the kitchen was warm and nobody asked her to be anyone she wasn't.

Babička is still the safe place but the safe place is slower now. She notices things she doesn't want to notice — the way Babička's hands shake when she cuts apples, the way she forgets which drawer the tea is in, the way she says Anna's name and then pauses like she's checking she got it right. The catalogue of decline lives in Anna's stomach next to all the other fears. She hates that she's tracking it. She hates that part of her is already preparing for the next loss. Babička's kitchen is still warm and undemanding but the warmth is thinner somehow, like the house is letting in a draft that wasn't there before.

Franti\u0161ek Novotn\u00fd.notes: He went from the quiet man in the corner who smelled like tobacco to the only calm thing in the house. The chess games did it — not because he was teaching her strategy, but because he was teaching her patience, and patience from a man who moved slowly and spoke rarely was different from patience from a woman who moved quickly and said 'proboha.' Děda never asked how she was feeling. Děda never checked if she was okay. Děda just set up the pieces and said 'tvoje tah' — your turn — and let her take it. She didn't know this was love. She didn't know that some people say 'I love you' by sitting across from you for three hours without needing anything back. She didn't know he was sick. She thought the naps were normal. She thought the slowness was just age. She thought they had time.

He went from present to absent in the span of weeks but the teaching continued without him. She hears 'dobře, dobře' when she makes a good decision. She took the chess set to the attic and put it on the shelf where she can see it from the bed — not to play, she can't play, the thought of moving those pieces without him makes her throat close — but to see. To remember that patience existed. That someone sat with her for hours without needing anything back. The dead don't leave. They just stop being in the room. She didn't know this before. She knows it now.

He went from present to absent in weeks but the teaching continued without him. She hears 'dobře, dobře' when she makes a good move, not his voice, just the shape of his approval, the memory of it. The chess set on the shelf is a gravestone she can see from her bed. She can't play. The thought of moving those pieces without him makes her throat close. But she looks at it every night. She doesn't know if this is grieving or holding on or both. She doesn't know the difference.

Si\u00e2n Liebig.notes: She became real. Not a person — Anna still hadn't seen her face — but a fact. A force. A name that lived in the flat like mould, growing in the corners of conversations, appearing without warning. 'Your father and Siân.' 'Siân's probably got a nice flat.' 'Siân this, Siân that.' Anna drew her in her head: tall, thin, mean-looking, the kind of woman who took things. The villain in a story that was supposed to be about her family. She didn't know yet that hating someone you've never met is exhausting. She just knew the name made her stomach clench and her hand reach for the skin around her thumbnail.

The villain died in the doorway of the Lebanon Road flat. The tall mean woman who stole Dad — that Siân never existed, was never real, was a story Anna told herself because stories with villains have endings and the truth didn't. The real Siân was short and nervous and touched her coffee stain and laughed at the wrong moments and asked about school in a voice that was trying so hard it made Anna's teeth hurt. You can hate a villain. You can't hate a woman who is clearly terrified of you, clearly trying, clearly aware that she's standing in the middle of someone else's family and doesn't know where to put her hands. Anna didn't forgive her. Forgiveness wasn't the right word. She just couldn't sustain the hatred anymore and the hatred had been useful — it was a clean feeling, a simple feeling, and without it she had to feel the complicated thing underneath which was that Siân wasn't the problem, had never been the problem, the problem was Dad leaving and you can't hate someone for leaving if you also understand why they left and she didn't want to understand but she was starting to.

Poppy Liebig.notes: The half-sister went from abstract to concrete — blue eyes, grabbed fistfuls of Anna's hair, said 'Anna' in the English toddler way that makes every name sound like a question. She didn't feel sisterhood. She didn't feel jealousy, not exactly. She felt like she was looking at evidence: this is what he does, he builds, he makes people, he leaves them in clean flats without knocking radiators and he visits on Sundays and says 'alright, Munch?' and the world keeps spinning. Poppy was three and didn't know any of this and that was the worst part, the not-knowing, the innocence, the fact that this small blue-eyed person would grow up with the easy version and never know there had been a hard version. Anna looked at her and chose nothing. The nothing cracked later, in the attic room, at 3am, when she imagined Poppy older, Poppy at fourteen, Poppy sitting on someone's shoulders, and the crack let something in that she still can't name.

Mark\u00e9ta Dvo\u0159\u00e1kov\u00e1.notes: She became the first Czech person outside the family, the first proof that Czech existed beyond the walls of the flat. She came with koláče and she spoke fast and she didn't treat Anna like she might shatter. Anna couldn't know this yet, but Markéta was the first adult in her life who wasn't afraid — not of the baby, not of the damp, not of the radiator, not of the English world outside the door. She just held her and talked and the baby learned that some chests were relaxed, some voices were easy, some people didn't carry the weight of two countries on their shoulders.

Krish Patel.notes: He became her first violence. She doesn't remember him — his face, his voice, why he was near her. He exists only as a story told at family gatherings, Klára's voice saying 'Anna always had a temper' with that laugh that isn't really a laugh, and Anna wanting to scream that she doesn't remember, that she isn't that girl, that the bite came from a body that wasn't her yet. Krish Patel probably has a small scar. Krish Patel probably doesn't remember either. They were both two. But in family mythology she is the biter, the angry one, the one who couldn't handle nursery, and the story is told about her not to her and it will never leave.

Miss Okonkwo.notes: She became the first adult outside the family who treated Anna like she was capable instead of fragile, and the impact of this was enormous and invisible. Miss Okonkwo didn't hover. Didn't check if Anna was breathing. Didn't hold her too tight. Just expected her to do the work, and Anna did the work, and the simplicity of that exchange was like water after weeks of juice — too sweet, juice, you forget what plain tastes like. She started walking near Miss Okonkwo at playtime. Not talking. Just near. Like the fish tank at nursery but warmer.

The Radiator.notes: It wasn't just the angry man anymore. The radiator pipe was a wiretap, a confession box, a thing that carried the kitchen into her bedroom. The same pipe that knocked carried voices. The same metal that scared her with its fist-sounds now scared her with its whispers. She started pulling her pillow over her head when it knocked, not because the sound was loud, but because the sound meant something was coming — arguments, Czech words she couldn't translate, the sound of her parents becoming people who fought in kitchens after bedtime. The radiator was the enemy now. The man inside knew things she shouldn't know.

Elara Chung.notes: She became the escape hatch. The first person outside the flat who felt like shelter, who didn't need explanations, who just let Anna exist without asking her to translate herself. The backwards language was a country nobody else could enter. Elara's flat was bigger and nobody cried in the bathroom and nobody had a spare room with a closed door. Anna told Elara that Dad moved into the spare room — 'he snores too loud' — but not about the crying, not about the pipe, not about the arguments the radiator carried into her bedroom. She kept the flat's secrets inside the flat where they belonged. Elara never pushed. That was the thing. Elara just made space and Anna filled it or didn't, and the not-having-to-explain was the closest thing to peace she'd felt since the slippers moved.

She became the only person outside the flat who knew the shape of the thing, not the details — not about the crying or the radiator or the spare room — but the fact of it, the before and after. The friendship bracelet was a contract written in thread: I know and I'm here and I won't make you explain. Anna didn't have to perform okay-ness with Elara the way she did at home with Mum or at Nando's with Dad. Elara made space and Anna filled it or didn't, and the not-having-to-perform was the only place she could breathe.

The goodbye was too big for two ten-year-olds and they performed it like they understood what 'never seeing each other at school again' meant, but they didn't, not yet. The pizza, the crying, the bracelet — it was all correct, all the right beats, but underneath it was the panic of two kids who had never lost anyone trying to figure out the choreography of loss. The FaceTime calls went from daily to weekly to monthly to a happy birthday text, and the friendship bracelet is still on her wrist, grey now, and the friendship isn't dead but it's not alive either, it's somewhere in between, in the space where daily used to be. The friendship didn't end. It just thinned. Like the thread on the bracelet, like the signal, like all the things you promise to keep when you're crying and the promise feels like the only thing keeping the world together.

Tereza B\u00e1rtov\u00e1.notes: Became the enemy by accident, by one word, by the casual cruelty of children who don't know they're being cruel. 'Angličanka' — the English girl — whispered on the second day, loud enough for the others to hear, and the laugh that followed, and the face attached to the laugh, brown hair, sharp chin, eyes that found Anna's and held them for a beat too long. The toilet incident cemented it. The sound Tereza made — that intake of breath, that bright sharp laugh — it lived in Anna's chest like a splinter. She hated Tereza with a clean hatred, uncomplicated, the hatred of someone who has been marked as different and then been made to feel it. She didn't know this would become the closest friendship she'd ever have. She couldn't know. She only knew that Tereza Bártová was the face that laughed when she was drowning.

The shift began in the canteen, at the table near the bins, in twenty minutes of silence. Before that, Tereza was the laugh in the toilet, the 'angličanka,' the face that found hers and held it too long. After that, Tereza was the girl who knew that silence was better than sorry. Anna started noticing her differently — not as the enemy but as someone who understood something about grief without being told. The foundation was laid. Not friendship yet. Something underneath friendship. The knowledge that this person would sit with you in the worst moment and not make it worse. That's rare. Anna didn't know it was rare. She was eleven. She knows now.

The bathroom rewired everything. Tereza was the laugh in the toilet, the angličanka, the face that found hers and held it too long — and then she was the hand that slid a pad under the door without being asked, without making it weird, without collecting the debt. The canteen silence sealed it. Twenty minutes of not-talking that said more than any apology could have. Anna didn't forgive Tereza — forgiveness felt too clean, too simple, like erasing something that should stay visible. But she let the cruelty and the kindness exist in the same person, and that was new, that was adult, that was the first time she understood that people could be more than one thing. The café on Náměstí became their country — the back corner, the free hot chocolate from Tereza's mum, the mixed Czech-English that was theirs and no one else's. Tereza never mentioned the bathroom again. Anna never mentioned the angličanka. The past existed between them like a scar — visible, permanent, but no longer bleeding.

From closest friend to ride-or-die, which is a distinction that sounds the same but isn't. The Ondřej thing sealed it. Tereza said no to a boy she might have said yes to because Anna liked him, and that kind of loyalty doesn't come with instructions for gratitude, it just sits there, real and heavy, changing the weight of everything. They started speaking in the mix more — Czech and English tangled together, codeswitching mid-sentence, creating a language that was theirs and no one else's. 'He's such a dick, ale vlastně ne, he's just oblivious, like properly oblivious, úplně mimo.' Anna told Tereza about the hair-flip and Tereza laughed so hard she choked on her hot chocolate and then said 'that's the worst thing I've ever heard' with such genuine admiration that Anna almost felt okay about it. Almost. The friendship had a scar now — the angličanka, the bathroom — and the scar was visible and permanent but the tissue around it was stronger than before, and the Ondřej situation was the thing that proved it.

The voice note could have broken it. 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' — the most honest thing Anna had said about their friendship and it was broadcast to seven people who weren't Tereza. But Tereza found it funny, actually funny, said 'you're such a disaster' with the fondness that Anna couldn't parse, and the friendship bent but didn't break because Tereza understood something that Anna didn't: that being annoyed by someone you love isn't betrayal, it's just being a person. The tension didn't disappear. Anna was more careful now, filtered her complaints, made sure the right name was at the top of the screen before she hit send. But something loosened too — Tereza had heard the 'I love her' and hadn't flinched, and being loved by accident was different from being loved on purpose, and maybe more real.

From closest friend to the only person who sees the whole map. The voice-note disaster could have broken them — 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' broadcast to seven strangers — but Tereza laughed, actually laughed, said 'you're such a disaster' with the kind of fondness that made Anna's chest hurt. The friendship has scar tissue now (the angličanka, the bathroom, the voice note) and the tissue around the scars is stronger than before. Tereza doesn't try to fix the Mum stuff or the Dad stuff or the body stuff. She just sits in the café and steals marshmallows and says 'that's fucked' when things are fucked and that's the only kind of support Anna can tolerate. The code-switching is their private language now — Czech and English tangled mid-sentence, a border that belongs to no one but them.

Mat\u011bj Kr\u00e1l.notes: He went from a face in the corridor to the most important person she didn't choose. Before Konopiště: nothing. After Konopiště: the translator, the bridge, the person who sat next to her in class and whispered the words she needed. The Minecraft server made it solid — hours on Discord, building a city, her Czech getting faster, his English getting better. He never said 'angličanka' except as a joke between them, and the joke was theirs, not theirs-and-everyone-else's. She trusted him because he didn't expect anything back, but she doesn't know if that trust is real or just gratitude wearing a mask. She owes him more than she'd ever say out loud.

He became the outside world. The Discord calls stretched from an hour to three to six, the Minecraft server becoming a second country where she could build things and destroy things and the language didn't matter as much because they had a shared world that existed outside Benešov, outside Czech, outside the house with the closing walls. She told him things she didn't tell Mum — that she missed English, that the church bells still sounded wrong, that she dreamed about the radiator. He never made it weird. He just kept building and let her talk or not talk, and the not-having-to-perform was the same freedom she'd had with Elara Chung, but this time in Czech, which made it different, which made it feel like the language was becoming hers instead of being imposed on her. She trusted him because he didn't carry the anxiety that every other Czech person in her life carried. He was just a boy on a server, placing blocks, and she could be just a girl placing blocks next to him.

The ease curdled. Not all at once — there was no moment, no conversation, no confession — just the slow accumulation of looks that lasted too long and silences where silences hadn't been before. He started complimenting her in March, small things, 'nice run today' becoming 'you looked fast out there' becoming 'hezká' said to his mum when he thought Anna couldn't hear, and each one landed on her like a insect she wanted to brush off but didn't know how. The Discord calls continued — Minecraft, school complaints, the same rhythm — but now she was aware of her own voice in a way she hadn't been before, checking her words, checking his tone, checking the silence for something she didn't want to name. She missed the simplicity. She missed being just a voice on a server, just blocks and jokes, just Matěj who never looked at her like that. She didn't know how to make it go back and she didn't know if she wanted it to go back and both answers felt wrong so she pretended nothing had changed and the pretending was its own kind of distance.

The awkwardness hardened into architecture — a wall built from too-long looks and soft goodbyes and the way he says 'hezký' about things that aren't hezký, about things she built in Minecraft that are just blocks, just pixels, just the same rectangles they've always been. She logs on less now. The server feels like his territory suddenly, like his feelings have claim over the space they used to share. She misses the old Matěj — the one who translated the world, who placed blocks without breathing funny, who was just a boy and not a boy who liked her. She doesn't know how to grieve a friendship that's still technically alive. She doesn't know if she's allowed to mourn something that hasn't ended.

Mrs. Navr\u00e1tilov\u00e1.notes: She went from 'the teacher who didn't pity her' to 'the teacher who saw her.' The smile — January, maybe February, she can't remember — changed something. Anna started sitting closer to the front after that. Not at the back anymore. She didn't decide to move. Her body decided, the same body that bit Krish Patel, the same body that laughed in Czech — it moved toward Mrs. Navrátilová's smile like a plant toward light.

From 'the teacher who didn't pity her' to 'the teacher who saw her.' The smile — January, maybe February, she can't remember exactly, she thinks it was a Thursday — changed something. Anna started sitting closer to the front after that. Not at the back anymore. She didn't decide to move. Her body decided, the same body that bit Krish Patel, the same body that cried at 3am — it moved toward Mrs. Navrátilová's smile like a plant toward light, and she didn't question it because questioning it would mean admitting she'd been in the dark.

Karol\u00edna Dvo\u0159\u00e1kov\u00e1.notes: She went from a name on the results board to the only person who understood what running was for. Not exercise. Not sport. Not achievement. Running was the off switch. Karolína got that without being told — she never asked why Anna ran, never asked about feelings, never tried to make it about anything other than splits and race strategies and the clean mathematics of distance over time. The hug after the time trial was the first physical contact Anna had initiated or received that didn't come with anxiety attached. It was sincere. She didn't know how to do sincere but she tried, and Karolína seemed to accept the attempt, and the friendship built itself in motion, in intervals, in the shared language of lactic acid and personal bests.

From training partner to the only person who understood what running was for. The championship changed something — the hug after the race, the way Karolína looked at her when the results went up, not surprised, not pitying, just nodding like of course, like she'd known Anna could do it before Anna knew it herself. They don't talk about feelings. They talk about splits and race strategy and which spikes are best for mud and whether Coach Novotný is going soft in his old age. But after the race there was something new — a respect that was mutual and unspoken, the recognition of someone who had seen her at her worst (gasping, leg cramps, the time she cried after a bad time trial and pretended it was allergies) and at her best, and both were real. Anna doesn't have many relationships like that. Straight. Clean. No hidden rooms.

Ond\u0159ej Svoboda.notes: From a boy in the corridor to the centre of her chest to a splinter she learned not to touch. He asked Tereza out in May, maybe June, she can't remember the exact day but she remembers the corridor and Tereza's face doing something complicated when he walked away and Tereza turning to her and saying 'I said no, obviously,' and Anna saying 'good for you' in a voice that didn't sound like hers, flat and Czech and wrong. The 'good for you' lives in her mouth like a rotten tooth. She meant 'thank you' and she meant 'I hate that you knew before I told you' and she meant 'I'm so embarrassed I could die' and she said 'good for you' because the other things were too big and too true. She still thinks about him. Not every day anymore. Most days. The wanting is quieter now but it's still there, under her ribs, and when she sees him in the corridor she looks at the floor or her phone or anywhere else because looking at him reminds her of the hair-flip and the trip and the way he said 'you alright?' like she was nothing.

Pavel Sou\u010dek.notes: He went from invisible to intolerable the moment his coat appeared on the hook by the door. Before that he was just Mum's friend, just a name, just the guy from Mravní stavba who sometimes appeared at Babička's house with flowers that Anna ignored. But the coat changed everything. The coat meant staying. The coat meant his smell in the hallway, his shoes next to hers, his presence in the kitchen in the morning when she wanted it to be empty. He tried — God, he tried, with the trainers and the questions about school and the way he said 'dobrý den' every morning like he was asking permission to exist — and every attempt made it worse because every attempt was proof that he was trying to be something he wasn't, couldn't be, shouldn't be. The 'Dad' slip destroyed something. Not the relationship — there was no relationship to destroy — but the possibility of one, the tiny stupid part of her that had almost, for one second, felt something like ease. Now she couldn't look at him without seeing the hope on his face when the word came out, and the hope was worse than anything because hope meant he wanted it and she didn't want him to want it, she wanted him to stay in his lane, to be Mum's boyfriend and nothing more, to not try to be family because family was already full, family was already broken, there was no room.

Luk\u00e1\u0161 Fiala.notes: From a name on the class list to the boy who said 'nejhezčí holka' and got 'okay' for his trouble. He wasn't cruel, wasn't teasing, wasn't doing that thing boys do where they compliment you in front of their mates to prove they can. He was alone and nervous and genuinely meant it, and the genuine-ness was the problem because she knew what to do with boys who are dicks but she doesn't know what to do with nice. He said hi in the corridor after and she said hi back and then walked faster. He sat near her in the canteen once and she moved to sit with Tereza without explaining. She thinks about him at 3am — not fantasizing, exactly, more like replaying the moment and trying out different responses. 'Thank you.' 'That's nice.' 'You too.' Every version sounds worse than 'okay.' She can't solve him. He doesn't fit anywhere in the architecture she's built.
